Mohamed Abdelgawad
Chief Financial Officer (CFO)
DBA MBA CMA TOT
CFO with 17 years of hands-on experience in Accounting, Inventory Management, Cost Accounting, Financial Planning & Controlling, Forecasting & Budgeting, and Data Analysis. Now building practical SAP FICO expertise to bridge financial leadership with enterprise systems consulting.
What is SAP?

Definition

SAP stands for Systems, Applications & Products in Data Processing — a German ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) company founded in 1972. It allows organizations to manage all business operations from a single integrated system, covering accounting, inventory, HR, production, and more.

SAP is used by over 77% of Fortune 500 companies, making it the dominant ERP platform in large and mid-size enterprises worldwide.

ECC vs S/4HANA

SAP ECC — the traditional version currently running in most companies worldwide. Stable, widely known.

SAP S/4HANA — the next-generation platform built on SAP's in-memory HANA database. Faster, modern Fiori UI, and the future of SAP (ECC support ends 2030).

Why FICO?

FI/CO is the financial backbone of SAP. Every transaction in any other module — purchasing, sales, production — ultimately generates a financial posting that flows through FI.

FICO consultants are among the highest-demand and highest-paid SAP specialists globally.

SAP ECC vs S/4HANA — Comparison
FeatureSAP ECCS/4HANA
DatabaseAny RDBMSHANA only
SpeedStandardSignificantly faster (in-memory)
User InterfaceSAP GUISAP Fiori (web & mobile)
Accounting LedgerParallel ledgersUniversal Journal
SAP SupportEnds 2030Long-term future
Market AdoptionMajority currentlyGrowing rapidly

Lecture 1Introduction to SAP
ERP concepts, SAP history, products, roles, and system navigation

1. What is ERP?

ERP stands for Enterprise Resource Planning — a software system that integrates all core business processes (finance, procurement, sales, HR, production) into one unified platform.

  • All departments work on the same system and share the same data in real time
  • Eliminates data silos and manual re-entry between departments
  • Provides a single source of truth for management reporting and decision making
  • Examples of ERP systems: SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics, Odoo

2. What is SAP?

SAP is the world's leading ERP software, founded in 1972 in Walldorf, Germany by five former IBM engineers: Dietmar Hopp, Hans-Werner Hector, Hasso Plattner, Klaus Tschira, and Claus Wellenreuther.

  • SAP = Systems, Applications & Products in Data Processing
  • Used by over 440,000 customers in more than 180 countries
  • Runs in 77% of Fortune 500 companies
  • Headquartered in Walldorf, Germany — listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange

3. SAP Version History

R/1 1972 — First version, financial accounting only
R/2 1979 — Mainframe-based, multi-currency support
R/3 1992 — Client-server architecture, global success
ECC 6.0 2005 — Enhanced R/3, still most widely used today
S/4HANA 2015 — Latest generation, in-memory HANA database
⚠️ SAP will end support for ECC in 2030. All companies are expected to migrate to S/4HANA.

4. SAP Customers

SAP customers are the companies and organizations that purchase and use SAP software to run their business operations.

  • Examples: Siemens, Nestlé, Toyota, Shell, Unilever, Coca-Cola, BMW
  • In Egypt & Arab region: Aramco, telecom companies, banks, government entities, manufacturing groups
  • Customers can implement SAP directly or through a partner
  • After go-live, the customer's internal team manages the system day-to-day

5. SAP Partners

SAP Partners are certified companies that help customers implement, customize, and support SAP. They do not use SAP for their own business — they sell it and implement it for others.

  • Gold Partners / Platinum Partners — highest certification level, handle large enterprises
  • Examples: Deloitte, Accenture, IBM, PwC, Capgemini, HCL
  • Partners provide consultants who configure and deploy SAP at customer sites
  • Regional partners in Egypt and the Arab world handle local implementations
💡 As an SAP FICO Consultant, you would typically work for a partner company and be deployed to customer projects — or work directly in a customer's internal SAP team.

6. SAP S/4HANA

S/4HANA is SAP's latest-generation ERP platform, launched in 2015. The "S/4" stands for Suite for HANA.

  • Built on HANA — SAP's own in-memory database, significantly faster than traditional databases
  • Universal Journal — FI and CO are merged into one single table (ACDOCA), eliminating reconciliation
  • Simplified data model — fewer tables, faster processing, real-time reporting
  • SAP Fiori — modern web-based UI replacing the old SAP GUI (works on mobile and tablet)
  • Available as On-Premise (installed at company), Cloud, or Hybrid

7. SAP Products by Company Size

SAP offers different products depending on the size of the business:

SAP Business One
Small businesses (up to ~100 employees) — simplified, affordable, fast to implement
SAP ByDesign
Mid-size companies — cloud-based, pre-configured, subscription model
SAP S/4HANA
Large enterprises — full functionality, highly customizable, complex implementation
💡 S/4HANA can be sold and implemented by both Partners (for customers) and purchased directly from SAP. Business One and ByDesign are sold primarily through partners.

8. SAP GUI vs SAP Fiori

SAP GUI
  • Traditional desktop application
  • Used in SAP ECC and still in S/4HANA
  • Requires installation on each PC
  • Not mobile-friendly
  • T-Code based navigation
SAP Fiori
  • Modern web-based interface
  • Works on browser, tablet, mobile
  • No installation needed
  • Role-based — shows only what you need
  • Tile-based navigation (like a smartphone)

9. SAP Modules Overview

  • FI — Financial Accounting: GL, AP, AR, Asset Accounting, Bank
  • CO — Controlling: Cost Centers, Profit Centers, Internal Orders, Product Costing
  • MM — Materials Management: Purchasing, Goods Receipt, Inventory, Invoice Verification
  • SD — Sales & Distribution: Sales Orders, Delivery, Billing, Customer Management
  • PP — Production Planning: MRP, Work Orders, Bill of Materials, Capacity Planning
  • HR / HCM — Human Capital Management: Payroll, Time Management, Org Management
  • PM — Plant Maintenance: Equipment, Maintenance Orders, Breakdown Management
  • PS — Project System: WBS, Project Budgets, Milestone Billing
  • QM — Quality Management: Inspection Plans, Quality Certificates, Non-conformance
💡 All modules are integrated. Every transaction in MM, SD, or PP automatically generates a financial posting in FI — that's why FI is considered the backbone of SAP.

10. Consultant vs Key User vs End User

SAP Consultant

The technical and functional expert. Configures the system (SPRO), designs business processes, maps company requirements to SAP, trains key users, and supports go-live. Works for a partner company or as a freelancer. Needs deep SAP knowledge.

Key User

An experienced employee from the customer's side who knows both the business process and SAP well. Acts as the bridge between consultants and end users. Trains colleagues, handles basic issues, and communicates change requests to the IT/consultant team.

End User

A regular employee who uses SAP daily to do their job — posting invoices, creating purchase orders, entering timesheets, etc. Uses only the transactions relevant to their role. Does not configure or change anything in the system.

Lecture 2SAP Landscape & Implementation Methodology
Servers, SAP Activate, Blueprint, Org Structure, Master Data, Business Processes, WRICEF, FI & CO sub-modules

1. SAP Modules: Functional vs Technical

Functional Modules
  • Business-oriented modules
  • FI — Financial Accounting
  • CO — Controlling
  • MM — Materials Management
  • SD — Sales & Distribution
  • PP — Production Planning
  • HR — Human Resources
Technical Modules
  • IT & development oriented
  • ABAP — SAP's programming language (custom development, reports, enhancements)
  • Basis — System administration, servers, transport management, user access

2. SAP System Landscape — Three Servers

Every SAP implementation has at least three separate environments (servers) to ensure safe development and testing before anything reaches the live system.

DEV — Development Server
Where consultants configure and build. All changes start here.
QAS — Quality Assurance / Testing Server
Testing environment. Key users and consultants test all configurations here before go-live.
PRD — Production Server
The live system. Real company data. Only tested and approved changes are moved here.
Sandbox
Free training environment — used for learning and experimentation. No rules, no consequences.
💡 Changes always flow in one direction: DEV → QAS → PRD. You never make direct changes in PRD.

3. On-Premise vs Cloud

On-Premise
  • Physical servers located at the client's site
  • Company owns and manages the hardware
  • Higher upfront cost, more control
  • Common in large enterprises and government
Cloud
  • Private Cloud — dedicated servers for one company, hosted externally
  • Public Cloud — shared infrastructure (e.g. SAP BTP, AWS)
  • Subscription-based, lower upfront cost
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud is the main cloud offering

4. SAP Activate Methodology — 6 Phases

SAP Activate is the current official implementation methodology used for SAP projects (replaced the older ASAP methodology). It follows 6 sequential phases.

1
Discover
Sales team presents SAP to the potential customer. Demos, proposals, and scope definition. Project is sold and approved.
2
Prepare
Project kick-off. Teams are formed, infrastructure is set up, project plan is created, and access to systems is granted.
3
Explore → Blueprint
Workshops with the client to gather all requirements. Consultants document everything in the Blueprint. Phase ends with client sign-off on the blueprint.
4
Realize
Starts only after blueprint sign-off. Consultants configure the system, build WRICEF objects, run testing cycles, and train the client's team.
5
Deploy
Final preparation for go-live. Opening balances are loaded, final master data is migrated to PRD, and cutover plan is executed.
6
Run
Go-live. The system is now live. Consultants provide Hyper Care support (first few weeks). After stabilization, a support team takes over ongoing maintenance.
📌 SAP Activate replaced the old ASAP Methodology. If you see ASAP referenced, it is the legacy approach — Activate is the current standard.

5. The Blueprint Document

The Blueprint is the most important document in any SAP project. It is created during the Explore phase and describes every aspect of the implementation.

  • Documents all client requirements and how SAP will fulfill them
  • Serves as the contract between the partner (consultant) and the customer
  • Must be signed off by the client before Realize phase can begin
Blueprint Contents:
1. Organizational Structure — How the company's legal and operational structure maps to SAP
2. Master Data — All reference data: GL accounts, customers, vendors, cost centers, etc.
3. Business Processes — Every transaction flow documented end-to-end

6. SAP Organizational Structure (Full Hierarchy)

Client
The entire SAP system instance (e.g. Client 710 used in training)
Operating Concern (CO-PA)
Highest level in CO — used for Profitability Analysis (COPA). Groups multiple Controlling Areas.
Controlling Area
The CO management unit. Can contain one or multiple Company Codes. All cost centers and profit centers belong to a Controlling Area.
Company Code (e.g. 1000, 2000)
The legal entity — produces independent financial statements (Balance Sheet & P&L)
⚠️ Two reasons a Company Code may need a separate Controlling Area:
1. Different Fiscal Year (e.g. Jan–Dec vs Jul–Jun)
2. Different Chart of Accounts
Functional Area:

Used to classify costs and revenues by business function for segment reporting. Examples: Operations, G&A (General & Administrative), Sales. Enables reporting on revenue and expenses per functional area.

7. Master Data

Master Data is the core reference data that is created once and reused in all transactions. Unlike transactional data (invoices, payments), master data is stable and doesn't change often.

FI Master Data
  • Chart of Accounts (CoA) → G/L Accounts
  • Trial Balance accounts
  • Bank Accounts
  • Customer Master
  • Vendor / Supplier Master
CO Master Data
  • Cost Centers
  • Profit Centers
  • Internal Orders
💡 Reporting Structure Example: A company can be organized as Segments → Profit Centers → Cost Centers. Each Profit Center can have multiple Cost Centers beneath it, enabling layered reporting.

8. Core Business Processes in SAP

① Procure to Pay (P2P)

The end-to-end purchasing process — from buying goods/services to paying the vendor.

  • Purchase Requisition → Purchase Order
  • Goods Receipt (inventory updated)
  • Invoice Verification
  • Payment to Vendor
Modules involved: MM (procurement cycle + inventory) → FI (payment posting)
② Order to Cash (O2C)

The end-to-end sales process — from receiving a customer order to collecting payment.

  • Pre-sales activities (inquiry, quotation)
  • Sales Order creation
  • Delivery & Shipping
  • Billing (customer invoice)
  • Payment collection
Modules involved: SD (sales process) → FI (invoice & collection posting)

9. WRICEF — Custom Development Objects

During the Realize phase, consultants identify and build all custom objects needed beyond standard SAP. These are classified under the WRICEF framework.

WWorkflow — Automated approval processes and notifications
RReport — Custom reports built in ABAP beyond standard SAP reports
IInterface — Data exchange between SAP and external systems
CConversion — Data migration from old system into SAP
EEnhancement — Extending standard SAP functionality without modifying core code
FForms — Custom printed output: invoices, POs, statements
💡 A typical SAP project has around 20 WRICEF objects. The number and complexity of WRICEF directly impacts project cost and timeline.

10. Realize Phase — In Detail

Step 1 — Configuration: Consultants configure SAP in DEV using SPRO (IMG). All system settings, org structure, master data rules are set up.
Step 2 — Unit Test (Internal): Each consultant tests their own module independently. This is internal — done by the consulting team without the client.
Step 3 — Integration Test (External): All modules are tested together end-to-end. Done with the client's key users present. Tests cross-module flows (e.g. P2P: MM → FI).
Step 4 — Cycle Test: Full business cycle tested across all modules simultaneously to simulate real operations.
Step 5 — Training: Consultants train the client's key users on how to use the system.
Step 6 — UAT (User Acceptance Test): The client's team tests the full system themselves. All tested cycles are documented. Client provides final sign-off before go-live.

11. Deploy & Run Phases

Deploy
  • Load opening balances into PRD (trial balance, AR/AP balances)
  • Migrate final master data to the live system
  • Cutover from old system to SAP
  • System is ready for go-live
Run
  • System goes live — real transactions begin
  • Hyper Care period: consultants on standby for immediate support (first 4–8 weeks)
  • Go-live team handles any errors urgently
  • After stabilization → Support Team (consultants) takes over long-term

12. FI Sub-Modules

  • GL — General Ledger: Core of FI. All postings flow here. Produces Trial Balance and financial statements.
  • AP — Accounts Payable: Manages vendor invoices, payment runs, and vendor balances.
  • AR — Accounts Receivable: Manages customer invoices, collections, and customer balances.
  • Bank: House bank management, cash journal, and bank reconciliation.
  • Asset Accounting (AA): Fixed assets, depreciation runs, asset acquisitions and disposals.

13. CO Sub-Modules

  • Cost Center Accounting (CCA): Tracks costs by department or function. Answers: "How much did each department spend?"
  • Profit Center Accounting (PCA): Tracks revenues and costs by business unit. Answers: "Which segment is profitable?"
  • Internal Orders: Tracks costs for specific short-term activities or projects (events, campaigns, small capex).
  • Product Costing (CO-PC): Calculates the cost of manufactured products. Used with PP module.
  • Profitability Analysis (CO-PA): Analyzes profitability by customer, product, region, or sales channel.

14. Document Type

SAP stores each type of financial transaction in a separate document. Every journal entry is assigned a Document Type that identifies what kind of transaction it is.

  • Each Document Type has a unique 2-character key (e.g. KR = Vendor Invoice, DR = Customer Invoice, SA = G/L Account Document)
  • Controls the number range for document numbering
  • Every journal entry is assigned a unique document number linked to its document type
  • Examples: Customer invoices are stored separately from vendor invoices, which are stored separately from G/L entries
💡 Document Types make it easy to filter, search, and report on specific transaction types. You can pull all vendor invoices or all customer invoices separately.
Lecture 3GL Account Master Data
Fiori vs GUI hands-on · Authorization Matrix · Chart of Accounts · GL Account Creation (FS00) · Account Types · Reconciliation Accounts

1. Fiori vs GUI — Practical Comparison (24/4/2026)

The lecture started with a hands-on comparison between SAP Fiori and SAP GUI by opening the same transaction in both interfaces.

SAP Fiori
  • Modern web-based UI — runs in any browser
  • Open New Supplier Invoice app to see the difference in layout vs GUI
  • Edit the Home Page to add favourite apps (tiles)
  • Use the Fiori Library to search by T-Code and find the Fiori app name — e.g. search FB60 to get the equivalent Fiori app
SAP GUI
  • Classic desktop client — Windows-based
  • All transactions accessed via T-Codes
  • Investigate the Accounting Folder structure
  • User roles and authorizations managed via GUI
💡 T-Code tip: You can search any T-Code in Fiori Launchpad to find its equivalent Fiori app. For example, FB60 (Enter Vendor Invoice in GUI) maps to the "Create Supplier Invoice" tile in Fiori.

2. Authorization Matrix (FI)

SAP controls what each user can do through a structured Authorization Matrix. This is set up during the Blueprint phase and maintained by the Basis team.

Step 1 — Define FI Roles
Each role maps to a set of FI functions (e.g. post invoices, view reports, approve payments)
Step 2 — Assign Users to Roles
Each user is added to the relevant role(s) — done in GUI
Step 3 — Control by Function & Company
We can restrict each user within a specific function AND a specific company code
FI → External Reporting

Financial Accounting produces reports for external stakeholders — balance sheet, P&L, tax authorities

CO → Internal Reporting

Controlling produces reports for internal management — cost centers, profit centers, budgets

3. GL Posting — T-Code FB50

FI contains sub-modules: GL (General Ledger), AR (Accounts Receivable), AP (Accounts Payable), and more. We opened GL in GUI using FB50 to post a journal entry.

Document Date
The original document date
Example: 20/4/2026 (invoice date)
Posting Date
When we need to post it
The period it will affect in FI
Entry Date
System date auto-filled
The date we physically logged the entry
  • Enter Debit and Credit lines, Amount, and Currency
  • Any expense account must include a Cost Center — mandatory field
  • After posting, display the document to verify the entry was saved correctly
💡 FB50 is used for direct GL journal entries. For vendor invoices use FB60, for customer invoices use FB70.

4. Chart of Accounts (CoA)

The Chart of Accounts is the master list of all GL accounts in SAP. SAP uses a numeric coding structure — each account type has a defined range.

Account Type Range From Range To Sub-accounts Example
Assets 100000 199999 Current Asset (111xxx), Fixed Asset (122xxx)
Liabilities 200000 299999 —
General / Equity 300000 399999 —
Revenue 400000 499999 —
Expenses 500000 599999 —
SAP Secondary Group 99999 — Internal SAP use only — not visible to company team
  • SAP team uploads the full CoA via a Cockpit file during implementation
  • Alternatively, use FS00 to create GL accounts manually one by one
  • In FS00, the left panel shows the CoA of the company — e.g. Fixed Asset → Land

5. GL Account Types

When creating a GL account in FS00, you must define the GL Account Type. This determines how the account behaves in postings.

Balance Sheet Account
  • Assets, Liabilities, Equity accounts
  • Balances carry forward to next fiscal year
Primary Cost / Revenue
  • P&L accounts linked to CO cost elements
  • Requires Cost Center on every posting
Non-Operating Expense / Income
  • Other income/expenses not part of core operations
  • No Cost Center required
Cash Accounts & Secondary Costs
  • Cash accounts: related to bank accounts
  • Secondary costs: internal SAP allocations only (99999 range)
CElem Category — What it is and why it matters

When you create a Primary Cost / Revenue (Type P) GL account, SAP asks for a CElem Category (Cost Element Category) on the Controlling tab. This links the GL account to the CO module and controls what kind of costs or revenues can flow through it.

Category Meaning Typical GL accounts
1Primary CostsAll expense accounts — salaries, rent, utilities, cleaning
11RevenueRevenue / income accounts that feed CO-PA
12Sales DeductionsDiscounts and rebates on sales
⚠️ CElem Category is mandatory for Account Type "P". Without it, the GL account cannot be saved. In the FS00 exercise you will see this field on the Control Data tab under "Account Settings in Controlling Area". It is automatically proposed when you enter the Controlling Area.

6. Bank GL Accounts — Cash Account Type

Cash Account type is used for bank-related GL accounts. In SAP, GL bank accounts work with currency — each currency gets its own set of GL accounts.

Example — EGP Bank GL Structure
EGP Currency
GL Main — Bank main account
Check In / Check Out
Transfer In / Transfer Out
  • We select the bank account together with the currency
  • Each currency creates its own GL accounts — e.g. EGP has its own set, USD has its own set
  • Important: We do NOT create a separate GL for every individual bank account — we create GL accounts per currency

7. Creating a GL Account in FS00 — Key Fields

When creating a new GL account, FS00 has three main pages (tabs). Here are the essential fields on each page.

Page 1 — Type/Description
GL Account Type
Balance Sheet / P&L / Cash / Secondary
Account Group
Select from CoA — e.g. Fixed Asset, Revenue
Short Text
Brief account name (shown in lists)
Long Text
Full description of the account
Page 2 — Control Data
Account Currency
The currency this GL will post in
Tax Category
Which tax types are allowed (e.g. "All types" or "Post without tax")
Posting Without Tax Allowed
Checkbox — allows posting with no tax code
C.Elem Category (CO Element)
1=Expense, 11=Revenue, 12=Sales Deduction
Recon. Account for Account Type
Links this GL to a sub-ledger (e.g. Customers, Vendors). When selected — direct journal entries are blocked; you MUST post via the sub-ledger (e.g. must select a Vendor when posting to a Vendor recon. account)
Page 3 — Create/Bank/Interest
Field Status Group
Defines which fields are mandatory / optional / hidden when posting to this account (e.g. Text, Value Date)
Post Automatically Only
Checkbox — account can only be posted by system automatically (e.g. bank clearing accounts)
Planning Level
Related to Cash Flow Forecasting in SAP
💡 Tip: Instead of creating a GL account from scratch, you can copy from an existing account and edit it — faster and avoids missing fields.

8. Reconciliation Accounts — Key Concept

A Reconciliation Account is a GL account that is linked to a sub-ledger (AR, AP, Asset Accounting). This is one of the most important concepts in FI.

⚠️ Rule: No direct posting to Recon. Accounts

If a GL account is flagged as a Reconciliation Account → you cannot post a manual debit/credit journal entry directly to it. You must post through the sub-ledger transaction.

Example:
  • GL account is a Vendor Reconciliation Account
  • You cannot post a direct journal entry to it (Dr / Cr)
  • You must use FB60 (Enter Vendor Invoice) and select a specific Vendor
  • SAP then automatically posts to the Recon. account behind the scenes
Lecture 4GL Account Posting & Document Entry
CoA review · FS00 hands-on examples · Account functions · FB50 & F-02 posting · Posting Keys · Practical exercise

1. Chart of Accounts — Quick Review

SAP supports three types of Chart of Accounts assigned at different levels. All three may exist in a single system simultaneously.

Operating CoA

The main chart used for all daily postings — mandatory. Every Company Code must have one Operating CoA assigned.

Country CoA

Maps accounts to local legal reporting requirements (e.g. Egyptian tax authority chart of accounts). Optional — used when local laws require a different numbering structure.

Group CoA

Used for consolidated financial reporting across multiple Company Codes or legal entities within a group. Optional — used by holding companies.

2. FS00 — Creating a GL Account (Hands-On)

T-Code: FS00 → Create, Change, Display GL Account Master Data

💡 Workflow: Enter T-Code FS00 → Enter account number → Enter Company Code → Click Create → Fill in all required fields → Save
★ Example 1 — Create a Revenue Account
Field Value Notes
GL Account480001Account number (range 400000–499999 = Revenue)
Company CodeBEV1The company this account belongs to
Account TypePPrimary Costs or Revenue (P&L account)
Account GroupPLP&L Statement Accounts group
Short TextCash RevenueShown in search lists and reports
Long TextCash RevenueFull description
Account CurrencyEGPMain posting currency
Tax Category*Asterisk = All tax types allowed
Posting w/o Tax Allowed✓ CheckedAllows posting without a tax code
CE Element Category1111 = Revenue (CO integration)
Field Status GroupG001General — standard field layout for posting
★ Example 2 — Create a Fixed Asset Account
Field Value Notes
GL Account122224Range 120000–129999 = Fixed Assets
Company CodeBEV1The company this account belongs to
Account TypeXX = Balance Sheet Account
Account GroupASFixed Asset Accounts group
Short / Long TextCars 2Account description
CurrencyEGPMain posting currency
Tax Category*All tax types allowed
Field Status GroupG001General field status

3. ⚠️ Common Error — "Post Auto Only" + Reconciliation Account

During practice in FS00, a common error appears when two conflicting settings are activated simultaneously on the same GL account.

❌ The Conflict:
  • Post Automatically Only — prevents manual journal entries; only the system can post to this account
  • Reconciliation Account for Account Type — also prevents direct manual posting (by definition)
  • SAP issues an error because both settings block direct posting — they are redundant and contradictory when used together
✅ How to Fix It:
Case 1: You want a Reconciliation Account

Remove "Post Auto Only" — the Recon. Account already blocks direct posting automatically. No need to add Post Auto Only.

Case 2: You want system-only posting

Use "Post Auto Only" only — do NOT set Reconciliation Account type. Used for clearing accounts (e.g. GR/IR account).

4. GL Account Management Functions

From within FS00, the following management actions are available on any GL account:

Block
Temporarily freezes the account. No new postings can be made to a blocked account. Used when an account needs to be reviewed or is no longer in use. Reversible at any time.
Select for Deletion
Marks the account for deletion. The account is not deleted immediately — it is flagged and physically removed later by a separate archiving run. The account must have zero balance and no open items.
Change Company Code
Transfer or update the Company Code-level settings for this GL account. Useful when extending an account to a new Company Code without recreating it from scratch.
T-Code: FS15 — Copy GL Account Master Data

Use FS15 to copy an existing GL account's master data to a new account number. This saves time and ensures consistency — instead of entering all fields manually, you copy from a similar account and adjust only what needs to change.

5. GL Document Posting — T-Code FB50

T-Code: FB50 → Enter G/L Account Document (modern interface)

FB50 is the standard transaction for posting manual journal entries directly to GL accounts. It consists of two parts: Header Data and Line Items.

Part A — Document Header
Document Date
Date of the original source document
e.g. invoice date, receipt date
Posting Date
The date posted to the accounting period
Determines which fiscal period is affected
Reference
External reference number
e.g. invoice number, contract number
Document Header Text
Free-text description of the document
e.g. "Monthly rent payment — May 2026"
Part B — GL Account Field (Line Items)

In the line items area, you search for the GL Account by description or account number. SAP allows multiple search methods (F4 help): by account number, short text, or long text.

Part C — Action Buttons (Top of Screen)
Simulate
Preview the journal entry (Debit = Credit check) before posting. No document is created. Use this to verify the entry is correct.
Hold
Save the entry as a personal draft without posting. Only the same user can retrieve and continue it. Does not appear in any reports or balances.
Park
Save the document in a shared draft state. Other users (e.g. supervisor) can open, review, and complete it. Appears in parked document reports but does not update GL balances yet.
Post
Final, irreversible posting. Creates a permanent Document Number, updates all GL balances, and the entry becomes part of the audit trail. Cannot be deleted — only reversed.
Save as Completed
Equivalent to Post — saves and posts the document as final. Used when the entry is complete and ready for immediate posting.
⚠️ Hold vs Park — Key Difference: Hold = private draft (only you can open it). Park = shared draft (any authorized user can open, edit, and post it). Both do NOT update GL balances.

6. T-Code F-02 — Classic GL Posting Interface

F-02 is the classic (legacy) interface for GL postings. It is equivalent to FB50 but provides additional options — particularly manual entry of Posting Keys, which gives more control over the document line items.

💡 F-02 = FB50 + manual Posting Key entry + extra options. Most consultants use FB50 for standard entries and F-02 when specific Posting Key control is needed.
Header Data in F-02:
Field Description
Document DateDate of the original source document
Posting DateDate the entry affects the accounting period
Document TypeDefines the transaction type — e.g. SA = General G/L posting, KR = Vendor Invoice, DR = Customer Invoice
Company CodeThe Company Code to post under
PeriodAuto-calculated from Posting Date (e.g. May 2026 = Period 5)
CurrencyCurrency of the transaction
Document NumberCan be entered manually or auto-assigned by SAP based on Document Type number range
ReferenceExternal reference (invoice number, PO number)
Document Header TextFree description of the document

7. Posting Keys (PstKy)

A Posting Key is a 2-digit code entered in the line item of a document. It defines:

  • Account Type — which sub-ledger (Customer, Vendor, GL, Asset)
  • Debit or Credit — the direction of the posting
  • Transaction type — the business context (invoice, payment, etc.)
💡 Memory tip: Customer keys start with 0 (01, 05, 09, 11, 15) · Vendor keys start with 2–3 (21, 25, 29, 31, 35) · GL keys are 40/50 · Asset keys are 70/75
PstKy Account Type Dr / Cr Transaction
01CustomerDrInvoice — enter Customer Code; SAP auto-adds Recon. GL account
05CustomerDrDebit Memo — customer (used to reduce a customer's balance; e.g. a returned goods credit note issued by the customer)
09CustomerDrSpecial G/L — Debit
11CustomerCrCredit Memo
15CustomerCrIncoming Payment
21VendorDrCredit Memo
25VendorDrOutgoing Payment
29VendorDrSpecial G/L — Debit
31VendorCrInvoice
35VendorCrIncoming Payment
40G/L AccountDrDebit Entry
50G/L AccountCrCredit Entry
70AssetDrDebit Asset (Acquisition / Addition)
75AssetCrCredit Asset (Disposal / Depreciation)
8. Tax Codes & Gross vs Net Posting

Every GL posting that touches a tax-relevant account requires a Tax Code — a 2-character key that tells SAP which tax rate applies and how to calculate it. In the BEV1 training environment the two most common codes are:

Code Name Rate When to use
V0Input Tax — 0%0%Tax-exempt transactions; also used when the GL account is flagged "Posting without tax allowed"
V1Input Tax — 14%14%Standard taxable vendor purchases (Egypt VAT rate); SAP auto-calculates and posts to the tax GL account
Gross vs Net Posting

When you enter an amount in FB50 or FB60 you must tell SAP whether the amount is gross (tax included) or net (tax excluded):

  • Gross posting (default): You enter the total invoice amount. SAP calculates the tax portion and splits it automatically — expense line = net amount, tax line = tax amount.
  • Net posting: You enter only the net amount. SAP adds the tax on top. Less common; requires the "Net Document" checkbox or the relevant field-status setting.

Example: Invoice EGP 11,400 gross (EGP 10,000 net + EGP 1,400 VAT at V1 14%). In gross mode, enter 11,400 — SAP posts: Expense Dr 10,000 / Tax GL Dr 1,400 / Vendor Cr 11,400.

💡 The Tax Category field on the GL master (FS00) controls which tax codes are allowed. An asterisk (*) means all types are permitted. If the field is blank, no tax code can be entered for that account.
Lecture 5Document Management & Reporting
Command field navigation · FB03 · FB08 · FBV0 · FBL3N · FAGLB03 · SU01 · Fiori Upload · FBCJ

1. SAP Command Field Navigation

The Command Field (the input box at the top-left of every SAP screen) is the fastest way to navigate. You can type a T-Code directly, or use special prefix commands to control how the next screen opens.

/o + T-Code
Open in a New Session Window
Opens the T-code in a brand-new window alongside the current one. Both stay open. Example: /of-03 opens F-03 in a new window without closing FB01.
/n + T-Code
Replace the Current Window
Closes the current screen and opens the new T-code in the same window. Example: /nf-03 closes FB01 and opens F-03 in its place.
/o
Open a New Empty Session
Opens the ABAP Sessions dialog showing all open sessions. You can create a new blank session or delete an existing one. SAP allows multiple sessions at the same time.
/n
Close the Current Window
Closes the currently open transaction and returns to the SAP Easy Access home screen. No data is saved.
/nex
Exit / Close the SAP System
Logs out and closes the entire SAP session completely.
💡 Quick tip: You can type any T-Code directly in the command field (e.g. fb03) and press Enter to open it in the same window — no prefix needed for basic navigation.

2. FB03 — Display Document

FB03 is the main T-code for finding and displaying posted accounting documents. It is one of the most frequently used T-codes for any FI consultant or accountant in SAP.

A. Document Search (Document List)

From the FB03 initial screen, press the Document List button to search across all documents. The standard search filters include: Fiscal Year, Document Type, Posting Date, Entry Date, and Company Code.

  • Search results show: Company Code, Document Number, Year, Type, Currency, Doc Date, Posting Date, Entered On, User
  • Results can span hundreds of documents — use filters to narrow down
  • Click any document in the list to open and display it
B. Dynamic Selection — Search by User

The standard search screen does not show a User field. To search by user (or other additional fields), click the Dynamic Selection button. A panel appears on the left with extra filterable fields including User Name, Transaction Code, Changed On, and more.

💡 Example: tick User Name in the Dynamic Selection panel, enter FI.MAMA, and execute — the result shows only the 3 documents posted by that user.
C. Display Document — Data Entry View

After selecting a document, SAP opens the Data Entry View — showing the document header (number, date, reference, currency) and all line items with accounts and amounts. The toolbar includes:

  • General Ledger View button — switches the display to show GL accounts instead of customer/vendor account codes. Useful to see the underlying GL impact of the document.
  • Change Layout — customize which columns are visible in the line items table (add/remove Company Code, Posting Key, Tax Code, Clearing Doc, etc.)
  • Ctrl+Y — selects a specific cell in the document line item table
  • Export — exports the document data to Excel (.xlsx) or Word. SAP shows a "Select Spreadsheet" dialog before exporting.
D. Display Document Header

To view the full header information of a document, go to Document → Display Document Header (or press the header info button). The popup shows: Document Type, Reference, Document Date, Posting Date, Currency, Created By, Entry Date & Time, T-Code used to post, Logical System.

E. Document Environment — Accounting Documents

From the GL View, go to Environment → Document Environment → Accounting Documents. This shows a list of all related documents that were automatically generated when the original entry was posted — for example:

  • Accounting document (the main FI entry)
  • Profit center document
  • Controlling document
  • Profitability Analysis document (CO-PA)
📌 Example: when you sell an asset and post the entry, SAP automatically creates an additional Accumulated Depreciation entry for that asset. You can see this automatic entry via Environment → Accounting Documents.

3. FB08 — Reverse Document

FB08 is used to reverse (cancel) a posted document. In SAP, you cannot delete a document once it has been posted — the audit trail must remain intact.

⚠️ SAP Rule: You can never delete a posted accounting document. Instead, you record a reversal of the wrong document, which creates a mirror-image entry that cancels it out — then you post a new correct document.
  • Enter the Document Number, Company Code, and Fiscal Year of the document to be reversed
  • Enter a Reversal Reason (e.g. 01 = Reversal in current period) — this field is mandatory
  • Enter the Posting Date for the reversal entry
  • Click Post — SAP creates a new document that is the exact mirror of the original, effectively cancelling it
Additional Options in FB08
  • Display Before Reversal — view the document first before executing the reversal
  • Document List — view a list of documents to select for reversal
  • Mass Reversal — reverse multiple documents at once
Why This Matters
  • SAP maintains a complete, unbroken audit trail for compliance and reporting
  • Every reversal creates a traceable paper trail — both the original and the reversal remain in the system
  • Auditors can see the full history of corrections

4. FBV0 — List of Parked Documents

FBV0 is used to search for and manage parked documents. A parked document is an entry that has been saved as a draft but not yet posted — it does not update GL balances.

  • Search by Company Code, Document Number, Fiscal Year, Posting Date, Document Type, or Entered By user
  • The Processing Status section lets you filter by: Enter Release, Complete, or Released status
  • Open a parked document to review, edit, or complete it
Save as Completed → Approval Workflow

When you open a parked document and click Save as Completed, the document enters the approval workflow. It is now visible to authorized approvers who can review and post it. This is the standard process in companies with multi-level approval controls.

💡 Park vs Hold: Park (FBV0) = shared draft, visible to other authorized users. Hold = private draft, only accessible by the user who created it.

5. FBL3N — GL Account Line Item Display

FBL3N displays all individual line items (transactions) posted to a GL account. It is one of the main reporting T-codes for reviewing GL account activity.

  • Enter the G/L Account number and Company Code
  • Select the status of items to display: Open Items (not yet cleared), Cleared Items, or All Items
  • Set an Open at Key Date to see items that were open as of a specific date
  • Click Execute — results show: Document Number, Type, Date, Posting Key, Amount, Currency, Clearing Doc
Alternative T-Code: FAGLL03 — GL Account Line Item Display (GL View)

FAGLL03 provides the same line item display as FBL3N but from the General Ledger (GL) perspective. It shows additional GL-level fields and is the preferred T-code in S/4HANA environments. Both T-codes serve the same core purpose.

6. FAGLB03 — GL Account Balance Display

FAGLB03 shows the period-by-period balance of a GL account — not individual line items, but the aggregated debit, credit, and net balance for each fiscal period in the year.

  • Enter Account Number, Company Code, Fiscal Year, and Ledger (e.g. 0L = Leading Ledger)
  • Optionally check "Select Closing/Opening Postings at Year End"
  • Click Execute

The result shows a table with one row per fiscal period (1–16), displaying: Debit, Credit, Balance (net for the period), and Cumulative Balance (running total from period 1). The opening balance (carryforward from prior year) is shown at the top.

✅ Use case: FAGLB03 is ideal for quickly reviewing how a GL account's balance has moved across the year — e.g. checking if revenue or expense accounts are tracking correctly month by month.

7. SU01 — User Maintenance

SU01 is the T-code for managing SAP user accounts — creating users, setting passwords, and assigning roles that control what each user can access and do in the system.

Initial Screen
Enter the User ID (e.g. FI.MAMA) → click the Edit (pencil) icon or Display icon to open the user record. The Alias field shows the user's full name.
Address Tab
Contains the user's personal details: First/Last Name, Full Name, Department, Function, Language. Must be filled in when creating a new user.
Logon Data Tab
Set the user's password, user type (Dialog, System, Service, etc.), and validity dates (Start Date / End Date).
Roles Tab — Assign Roles
The most important tab for FI consultants. Add roles here to grant the user access to specific SAP functions. Each role has a Start Date and End Date (9999 = no expiry). Example: add SAP_BR_GL_ACCOUNTANT to give GL Accountant permissions.
💡 Role names in SAP follow a naming convention. SAP_BR_ prefix = SAP Best Practice Business Roles. Examples seen in class: SAP_BR_CASH_MANAGER, SAP_BR_CASH_SPECIALIST, SAP_BR_INVENTORY_MANAGER, SAP_BR_GL_ACCOUNTANT.

8. Fiori — Upload General Journal Entries

SAP Fiori provides an app for mass-uploading GL journal entries from an Excel or CSV file — useful when you need to post many entries at once (e.g. period-end allocations, opening balances migration).

1
Open the App: From the Fiori Launchpad, search for "Upload General Journal Entries" and open it. The main screen shows a Browse button and a list of previous uploads.
2
Download the Template: Click "Download Template" at the bottom right. A dialog asks for language and format — choose Spreadsheet (.xlsx) or CSV. Download the file.
3
Fill in the Template: The template has a structured format with a Header section (Company Code, Journal Entry Type, Journal Entry Date, Posting Date) and Line Items section (Company Code, G/L Account, Item Text, Debit amount, Credit amount). Fill in your data — each journal entry needs at least one header and balanced line items.
4
Upload & Post: Click Browse, select the completed file, then click Post. SAP validates and posts all entries from the file. Use Show Log to review the result — any errors will be listed there.
📌 Template key fields: BUKRS = Company Code, BLART = Journal Entry Type, BLDAT = Journal Entry Date, BUDAT = Posting Date, HKONT = G/L Account, WRSOL = Debit Amount, WRHAB = Credit Amount.

9. FBCJ — Cash Journal

FBCJ is the T-code for the Cash Journal — used to record and review petty cash transactions (cash payments, cash receipts, and check receipts) directly in SAP.

  • On opening, SAP shows an Initial popup asking for Company Code and Cash Journal number — enter these to access the correct cash journal
  • The main screen has a Display Period section with shortcuts: Today, This Week, Current Period
  • The Balance Display section shows: Opening Balance, Total Cash Receipts, Total Check Receipts, Total Cash Payments, and the Closing Balance
  • Three tabs: Cash Payments | Cash Receipts | Check Receipts — each showing individual transactions in that category
  • Toolbar includes: Print Cash Journal and Change Cash Journal
💡 The Cash Journal in SAP is a real-time view of petty cash. Every transaction posted here updates the GL immediately — no separate reconciliation needed.

🗂️ L5 T-Code Quick Reference

FB03Display posted document — search, view, export
FB08Reverse a posted document
FBV0List and manage parked documents
FBL3NGL account line item display
FAGLL03GL line item display — GL view (same as FBL3N)
FAGLB03GL account balance display (period by period)
SU01User maintenance — create users, assign roles
FBCJCash journal — petty cash transactions
Lecture 6AP — Accounts Payable
Business Partner creation, Vendor Invoice entry, Cash Journal AP transactions, Vendor reporting T-Codes

1. Overview — AP Sub-Module

In this lecture we complete GL codes and begin AP (Accounts Payable) codes. AP manages all transactions related to vendors/suppliers — from creating vendor master data to entering invoices, making payments, and generating aging reports.

  • Vendors are called Suppliers or Business Partners in SAP S/4HANA
  • Every AP transaction posts automatically to the Reconciliation Account in GL (e.g. 210100 — Vendors Reconciliation)
  • AP sub-ledger keeps detail per vendor; GL keeps totals in the recon. account
  • Key document types: KR = Vendor Invoice, KZ = Vendor Payment
💡 In SAP S/4HANA, vendors and customers are unified under the Business Partner (BP) concept — one master record, multiple roles (General Partner, Supplier, Customer).

2. BP — Maintain Business Partner (Create Vendor)

Transaction BP is the central T-code for creating and managing Business Partners. To create a new vendor, you add the general person/org data first, then assign the Supplier role with FI and Purchasing data.

Step-by-Step: Create a New Vendor
  • Step 1 — Open BP: Type BP in the command field → the Maintain Business Partner screen opens with a Find/Worklist tab
  • Step 2 — Create as Person: Click Person in the toolbar → the "Create Person" screen opens
  • Step 3 — Set Grouping: In the Grouping dropdown, choose AF VENDOR FOR PG
  • Step 4 — Fill Name: Title → 0002 Mr. | First name → Gawad | Last name → Gawad
  • Step 5 — Street Address: Street/House number → Cairo | Postal Code → 11111 | City → cairo | Country → EG (Egypt) | Region → C (Cairo)
  • Step 6 — Check: Press Enter → status bar shows "Data of business partner is error-free"
  • Step 7 — Save: Click Save → SAP creates the Business Partner and assigns number (e.g. 446208). Status bar: "Business partner 446208 created"
📌 After saving, the screen switches to Display mode showing the new BP number. To add the Vendor role, you must go back into Edit/Change mode.
Add FI Supplier Role (FLVN00)
  • In the "Change in BP role" dropdown, select FLVN00 Supplier (Fin.Accounting)
  • Navigate to the Company Code section → Add Company Code: BEV1
  • In the Vendor: Account Management tab → enter Reconciliation Account: 210100 (Vendors Reconciliation)
  • Check → no error → Save → "Changes have been saved". Supplier number is now assigned (= BP number: 446208)
Add Purchasing Role (FLVN01)
  • Switch role to FLVN01 Supplier (New)
  • In the Purchasing Organization section → set Purch. Organization: BE25 (be7ery's po)
  • Check → no error → Save → "Changes have been saved". Role now shows as "FLVN01 Supplier (Maintained)"

3. FBCJ — Cash Journal (AP Tests)

In this lecture we also use FBCJ (Cash Journal) to practice AP-related cash transactions. The interface was already covered in L5 — here we focus on the AP-specific business transactions.

Test 1 — Payment to Vendor (Cash)
  • Open FBCJ → Cash Payments tab → click the Business Transaction field
  • From the search popup, choose PAYMENT TO VENDOR (Tran. 0007, type K)
  • Amount: 5,000 | G/L Account auto-fills to 210100 (Recon. Account) | Supplier: Behery (446031)
  • Save → Post → posted entry: Petty Cash 2 (110011) Cr 5,000 / Behery (446031) Dr 5,000
  • Click Follow-On Docs to see the generated accounting document
To Reverse the Transaction
  • Select the transaction row → click the (−) Reverse button in the toolbar
  • A "Reverse document?" popup appears → click Yes
  • A "Reason for reversal" dialog appears → enter Reversal reason: 01 | Posting Date: today → confirm
  • The journal now shows both the original (5,000) and the reversal (5,000−) — closing balance returns to opening balance
Test 2 — Expense (Cash)
  • Business Transaction: PURCHASES USING CASH | Amount: 8,000
  • G/L Account: 500002 (Electricity EXP) | Cost Center: INVCS01
  • Save → Post → Document: Petty Cash 2 (110011) Cr 8,000 / Electricity EXP (500002) Dr 8,000
Available Business Transactions in FBCJ
CASH SALE (0010)Type R — Revenue account 400000
CUSTODIES FROM (0014)Type K — Special G/L (A)
PAYMENT FROM CUSTOMER (0008)Type D
PAYMENT TO VENDOR (0007)Type K — Posts to recon. acct
PURCHASES USING CASH (0009)Type E — G/L 500000
REVENUE (0012)Type R — G/L 400000
TRANSFER BANK TO JOURNAL (0005)Type C — G/L 140100
TRANSFER JOURNAL TO BANK (0006)Type B — G/L 140100

4. FB60 — Enter Vendor Invoice

Transaction FB60 is used to enter a vendor (supplier) invoice in SAP. It posts automatically to the vendor sub-ledger and the reconciliation account (210100) in GL.

  • Open FB60 → "Enter Vendor Invoice: Company Code BEV1" screen opens
  • Vendor: enter vendor number (e.g. 580024) → a popup shows vendor address (Company: المتحدة, Cairo, Egypt)
  • Invoice date: the date on the vendor's invoice
  • Amount: total invoice amount (e.g. 8,000 EGP)
  • In the line items grid: G/L Account: 500005 (Cleaning Exp) | Cost Center: INVCS01
  • Simulate → verify the entry: Vendor (580024) Cr 8,000 | Cleaning Exp (500005) Dr 8,000
  • Post → SAP assigns Document Number (e.g. 1900000143, Doc Type KR)
  • Display → use FB03 to open and verify the posted invoice document
⚠️ FB60 vs MIRO — Know the difference: FB60 is a pure FI transaction — used when there is no Purchase Order (PO). It posts the vendor liability and expense directly. MIRO (Invoice Verification) is used when the invoice relates to a PO and requires a 3-way match: PO → Goods Receipt (GR) → Invoice. MIRO checks that the invoiced quantity and price match what was ordered and received. In your training environment you use FB60 because BEV1 exercises have no PO/GR flow. On a real implementation, most vendor invoices go through MIRO; FB60 is reserved for non-PO invoices (services, utilities, manual adjustments).
💡 Document Type KR = Kreditor Rechnung (German) = Vendor Invoice. The vendor account is automatically credited, and the expense GL account is debited.

5. FBL1N — Vendor Line Item Display

Transaction FBL1N shows all individual line items for one or more vendor accounts — open invoices, cleared payments, or all items.

  • Supplier Account: enter specific vendor number (e.g. 580024) or leave blank to view all
  • Company Code: BEV1
  • Line Item Selection — Status:
    • Open Items — invoices not yet paid (outstanding)
    • Cleared Items — invoices that have been paid/cleared
    • All Items — everything, paid or not
  • Execute → results show: Status (🔴 = open/overdue), Document Number, Type, Date, Amount, Currency, Text
Reading the Results
  • 🔴 Red (ST) — Open item (not yet paid)
  • 🔴 Red (⚡) — Overdue item (past due date)
  • 🟢 Green square — Cleared item (paid)
  • Doc Type KR → Vendor Invoice
  • Doc Type KZ → Vendor Payment
Key Date Fields
  • Baseline Date — the start date for calculating the due date (e.g. invoice date)
  • Payment Date / Net Due Date — the actual date payment is due
Special G/L Transactions

At the main selection screen, under Type, check the Special G/L Transactions checkbox. When you execute, the report shows a new column (S) and indicator (A) — these represent down payments made to the vendor. This allows you to see advance payments separately from regular invoices.

6. FK10N — Vendor Balance Display

Transaction FK10N shows the vendor account balance by period (monthly). Unlike FBL1N which shows individual line items, FK10N gives you a summary view — useful for reconciliation and reporting.

  • Enter: Vendor: 580024 | Company code: BEV1 | Fiscal year: 2026
  • Click Execute
  • The result shows a table with: Period (1–12), Debit, Credit, Balance, Cumulative Balance, Sales/Purchases
  • The Bals tab shows normal transactions; the Special G/L tab shows down payments
  • To drill down into a specific period's transactions, double-click on the amount in that period row → opens the Vendor Line Item Display (FBL1N) filtered to that period
💡 FK10N is the vendor equivalent of FAGLB03 (GL Account Balance Display). Use it to quickly see how much you owe a vendor across the year, period by period.

7. IDCNAP — AP Aging Report

Transaction IDCNAP generates the Accounts Payable Aging Report — it shows how long vendor invoices have been outstanding and groups them by aging buckets.

  • Supplier Selection: leave blank to include all suppliers, or enter a specific Supplier Account and Company Code range
  • Line Item Selection: Open Items at Key Date (e.g. today) — shows all unpaid invoices as of that date
  • Output Control → Due Date Sorted List: defines the aging buckets: 10, 20, 30, 60, 90, 180, 360, 720 days (1 Day intervals)
Reading the AP Aging Analysis
  • Columns: CoCd, Supplier, Supplier Name, G/L Account, Total Open, Not Due, <11 Days, 11–20 Days, 21–30 Days, 31–60 Days…
  • Each row shows a vendor with the total amount owed split across aging buckets
  • Example: Cocacola (Supplier 1) — Total Open: 2,800 — all in <11 Days (100%)
  • The report helps prioritize which vendors to pay first based on how long invoices have been outstanding
📌 The AP Aging Report is one of the most important reports for a CFO or AP manager — it shows the total outstanding liability by vendor and by aging period at any key date.

🗂️ L6 T-Code Quick Reference

BPMaintain Business Partner — create/edit vendors & customers
FBCJCash Journal — AP cash payments & expense postings
FB60Enter Vendor Invoice (AP document type KR)
FBL1NVendor Line Item Display — open, cleared, all items
FK10NVendor Balance Display — period-by-period summary
IDCNAPAP Aging Report — outstanding invoices by aging bucket
Lecture 7AP Down Payments & AR — Order-to-Cash
Vendor down payments & special G/L config, outgoing payments & clearing — then the start of AR: customer master, customer invoice, reporting, and incoming/down payments

1. Overview — Down Payments & the Move into AR

Lecture 7 completes the AP payment cycle with down payments, outgoing payments and clearing, then transitions into AR (Accounts Receivable) — the mirror image of AP on the customer side.

  • A down payment is an advance paid to a vendor before the invoice/goods arrive — tracked as a special G/L transaction, not a normal payable
  • Special G/L transactions post to an alternative reconciliation account, so advances are reported separately from regular invoices
  • Full AP advance cycle: request (F-47) → post down payment (F-48) → clear against invoice (F-54) → pay the balance (F-53)
  • AR handles the customer side — the Order-to-Cash (OTC) cycle — reusing the same logic with account type D instead of K
💡 Account type K = Kreditor (vendor) and D = Debitor (customer). Almost every AP transaction has a matching AR counterpart — e.g. F-53 pay vendor ↔ F-28 receive from customer.

2. F-48 — Post Vendor Down Payment

Transaction F-48 posts an advance payment to a vendor before the invoice arrives. It is recorded as a special G/L transaction (indicator A) so it shows separately from normal payables.

Data to Enter
  • Header: Document/Posting Date, Company Code BEV1, Currency, Reference, Document Header Text
  • Vendor data: Account (vendor number) and Special G/L indicator: A (down payment)
  • Bank data: House Bank, Account ID, Amount
  • Press Enter → the down payment line is generated against the alternative (special G/L) account → Post
  • Posting: Bank Cr / Vendor special G/L (down payment) Dr
💡 Because it uses special G/L indicator A, the advance lands on the down-payment recon account — keeping it out of the normal 210100 payables balance until it is cleared.

3. FBKP — Maintain Accounting Configuration (Special G/L)

Transaction FBKP is the configuration cockpit for automatic postings and special G/L. Here it defines which alternative G/L account a special G/L transaction posts to.

  • Open FBKP → choose the Special G/L selection
  • Select the Account type: K (vendor) — or D for customers
  • Select the special G/L indicator (e.g. A = down payment, F = down payment request)
  • Assign the special G/L account — e.g. the Down Payment account — against the normal reconciliation account
  • Save → from now on F-48 down payments automatically post to that account
📌 FBKP is configuration, not a daily transaction. The special G/L account must already exist (created in FS00 and flagged as a reconciliation account) before you can link it here.

4. F-47 — Down Payment Request

Transaction F-47 creates a down payment request — a noted item that records the intention to pay an advance, with no G/L posting yet. It later drives the actual payment.

  • Enter the header and vendor data, then add the requested amount
  • Special G/L indicator: F (down payment request)
  • Post → SAP records a noted item (statistical only — no real accounting entry, no balance change)
  • The request can then be picked up by the payment program or by F-48 to make the actual down payment
💡 A down payment request (indicator F) is a noted item — it appears in the vendor account as a reminder/trigger but does not affect the GL balance until an actual payment is posted.

5. F-53 — Post Outgoing Payment

Transaction F-53 pays an open vendor invoice. You enter the payment (bank) details, select the open invoice(s) to settle, and post — clearing the payable.

  • Main data: Document/Posting Date, Company Code BEV1, Currency
  • Bank data: bank/clearing G/L account and Amount paid
  • Vendor: enter the Account → Process Open Items
  • Select the invoice(s) so the assigned amount = the payment amount (the balance must be 0)
  • Post → Bank Cr / Vendor Dr; the paid invoice is now cleared (document type KZ)
💡 KZ = Kreditor Zahlung = vendor payment. After F-53 the invoice moves from Open to Cleared in FBL1N.

6. F-54 — Clear Vendor Down Payment

Transaction F-54 clears a posted down payment against the actual vendor invoice once it arrives — transferring the advance from the special G/L account back to the normal payable so the net remaining balance is correct.

  • Enter the header, Company Code BEV1 and the Vendor account
  • Select the relevant invoice the down payment relates to
  • Choose the down payment line(s) to clear (transfer posting)
  • Post → the special G/L down payment is reversed against the normal recon account; only the net amount remains open
📌 Sequence matters: post the down payment (F-48) → enter the vendor invoice (FB60) → clear the down payment against it (F-54) → pay the remaining balance (F-53).

7. AR — Accounts Receivable & the Order-to-Cash (OTC) Cycle

From here the lecture moves into AR (Accounts Receivable) — managing money owed by customers. The end-to-end flow is the Order-to-Cash (OTC) cycle.

The OTC Cycle
  • Create / maintain the customer master (BP)
  • Sales order → delivery → goods issue (the logistics/SD side)
  • Billing → customer invoice (FB70 in FI)
  • Customer pays → incoming payment (F-28)
  • Reporting: line items (FBL5N), balance (FD10N), aging (IDCNAR)
💡 AR mirrors AP: customer = account type D, customer invoice doc type DR, customer payment doc type DZ, and every transaction posts to the customer reconciliation account (trade receivables).

8. BP — Create New Customer

As with vendors, customers are created in the central BP (Business Partner) transaction. You create the partner, then add the FI customer role.

  • Open BP → create the partner → add Title, Name and the main/address data → Save
  • Add the FI role: press Change, then in "Change in BP role" choose FLCU00 — Customer (Fin. Accounting)
  • In the Company Code data, enter Company Code BEV1 and the customer Reconciliation Account (Trade Receivables)
  • Check → no error → Save
💡 FLCU00 = the FI customer role (the AR counterpart of FLVN00 for vendors). FLCU01 adds the Sales-area / SD customer data, just as FLVN01 adds purchasing data for vendors.

9. FB70 — Enter Customer Invoice

Transaction FB70 enters a customer (outgoing) invoice in FI — the AR counterpart of FB60. It debits the customer (receivable) and credits revenue.

  • Choose the Customer (e.g. CocaCola) → header shows the customer address
  • Enter the Invoice date and Amount
  • In the line items: G/L Account = Revenue 400000 with the amount
  • Add Profit Center and Profitability Segment as required for margin / segment reporting
  • Simulate → Customer Dr / Revenue Cr → Post (document type DR)
💡 DR = Debitor Rechnung = customer invoice. The customer account is debited (they owe us) and revenue is credited.

10. AR Reporting — FBL5N, FD10N, IDCNAR

The AR reporting trio mirrors the AP reports from L6, one-for-one — same screens, customer side.

FBL5NCustomer Line Item Display — open / cleared / all items (= FBL1N for vendors)
FD10NCustomer Balance Display — period-by-period balance (= FK10N for vendors)
IDCNARAR Aging Report — receivables by aging bucket (= IDCNAP for vendors)
  • FBL5N — line items per customer: status (open / cleared / overdue), document number, type, date, amount; supports the special G/L (down payments) view
  • FD10N — monthly Debit / Credit / Balance summary; double-click a period to drill into FBL5N for that period
  • IDCNAR — aging analysis (Not Due, <11, 11–20, 21–30, 31–60… days) per customer at a key date

11. F-28 / F-29 / F-39 — Customer Payments

The customer-side payment transactions, mirroring the vendor F-53 / F-48 / F-54.

F-28Post Incoming Payment — receive money & clear the open invoice (= F-53)
F-29Post Customer Down Payment — advance received (special G/L, = F-48)
F-39Clear Customer Down Payment — clear the advance against the invoice (= F-54)
F-28 — Steps
  • Enter the bank / clearing account and the amount received
  • Enter the Customer account → Process Open Items
  • Select the invoice(s) so the balance is 0 → Post (doc type DZ)
💡 Quick map — F-28 ↔ F-53 (payment), F-29 ↔ F-48 (down payment), F-39 ↔ F-54 (clear down payment). Same logic, customer side (type D) instead of vendor (type K).

12. Dunning — Automated Customer Payment Reminders (F150)

Dunning is SAP's automated process for chasing overdue customer invoices. Instead of sending manual reminder emails, the finance team runs a dunning run (F150) that identifies all overdue open items, assigns escalation levels, and generates dunning notices.

How Dunning Works
  • A Dunning Procedure (configured in FBMP) defines escalation levels — e.g. Level 1 = friendly reminder (5 days overdue), Level 2 = formal notice (15 days), Level 3 = legal warning (30 days)
  • The dunning procedure is assigned to the customer master (Company Code data). Without it, the customer is never included in a dunning run
  • Run F150: enter a Dunning Date, Company Code BEV1, select the dunning procedure → SAP scans all open items, identifies overdue ones, and proposes the dunning level
  • Review the proposal → Print/Issue notices — SAP can output by email, print, or EDI depending on configuration
  • A Dunning Block on the customer or on a specific invoice excludes it from the run (e.g. disputed invoice)
F150Execute Dunning Run — identify overdue items and generate notices
FBMPDefine Dunning Procedures — configure levels, days overdue, text
💡 Common dunning error: a customer receives a dunning notice for an invoice that was already paid — this happens when the payment was posted but not cleared against the invoice. Both the invoice and the payment remain as open items in FBL5N. Always run F-28 clearing after posting customer payments.

🗂️ L7 T-Code Quick Reference

AP — Down Payments & Payments
F-48Post Vendor Down Payment (special G/L ind. A)
FBKPMaintain Accounting Config — special G/L account setup
F-47Down Payment Request — noted item (ind. F)
F-53Post Outgoing Payment — pay & clear invoice (KZ)
F-54Clear Vendor Down Payment against invoice
AR — Order-to-Cash
BPCreate Customer — FI role FLCU00
FB70Enter Customer Invoice (DR)
FBL5NCustomer Line Item Display
FD10NCustomer Balance Display
IDCNARAR Aging Report
F-28Post Incoming Payment (DZ)
F-29Post Customer Down Payment
F-39Clear Customer Down Payment
F150Dunning Run — automated overdue customer reminder notices
FBMPDefine Dunning Procedures (configuration)
Lecture 8SAP FI Configuration
SPRO, Enterprise Structure, Global Settings, Document Types, Field Status, Tolerances & Account Groups

1. What is Configuration?

Configuration (also called Customizing) is the process of telling SAP how your company works — defining your legal entities, fiscal year, posting rules, and account structures. All configuration is done in a single tool called SPRO (SAP Project Reference Object, or the IMG — Implementation Guide).

What an End User does

Posts invoices, runs reports, processes payments. Uses T-codes like FB60, FBL1N, F110. Works in the live Production system (PRD).

What a Consultant does

Configures the system in SPRO. Defines rules the system will follow. Works in DEV first, transports changes to QAS for testing, then to PRD at go-live.

💡 Configuration is done in DEV → QAS → PRD. Never configure directly in PRD. Changes are moved using Transport Requests (T-codes SE09 / SE10).

2. SPRO — The Configuration Cockpit

T-code SPRO opens the SAP Reference IMG (Implementation Guide) — a structured tree of every configuration activity available in SAP. Think of it as a configuration menu organized by module and topic.

  • Navigate: SPRO → SAP Reference IMG → Enterprise Structure → Financial Accounting → ...
  • Each node in the tree links to a specific configuration transaction. You can also jump directly using the T-code (e.g. OBB8 for Payment Terms, OB52 for Posting Periods).
  • Configuration changes are automatically recorded in a Transport Request — a container that carries your changes from DEV to QAS to PRD.
  • You can also use SE09 or SE10 to manage and release transport requests.
⚠️ Every time you make a configuration change in DEV, SAP will ask you to assign it to a Transport Request. Never click "Local Object" for real project changes — that means it will never move to QAS or PRD.

3. Enterprise Structure — The Backbone of Every Configuration

Before any FI posting can happen, the organizational structure must be defined and linked together. This is the first thing a consultant configures on any project.

1
Define Company OX15
The highest legal entity — the holding company. Used for consolidated financial statements. Path: SPRO → Enterprise Structure → Definition → Financial Accounting → Define Company.
2
Define Company Code OX02
The smallest organizational unit for which independent financial statements are created. Example: BEV1 (be7ery). Each company code has its own currency, country, and language. This is the core FI unit.
3
Assign Company Code to Company OX16
Links the Company Code to the holding company. Required for group consolidation reporting. One company can contain multiple company codes.
4
Define Business Areas OX03
Optional — used for segment-level financial reporting across company codes. Example: Business Areas for different product lines or geographies. Not required for basic FI.
💡 In your training environment: Company Code = BEV1 (be7ery). You have used this in every posting exercise — FB60, FB50, FBL1N all required entering BEV1. Now you know that BEV1 was created in configuration during project setup.

4. Fiscal Year Variant — Defining Your Accounting Year

SAP does not assume January–December. You must configure the fiscal year variant that matches your company's accounting year and assign it to the company code.

Variant Description Example
K4Calendar year Jan–DecPeriod 1 = January, Period 12 = December. Most common worldwide.
V3Non-calendar year Apr–MarPeriod 1 = April, Period 12 = March. Common in India, UK.
CustomAny 12-month periodE.g. July–June. Defined by the consultant to match client's legal year.
  • Fiscal Year Variants are configured in OB29 (Maintain Fiscal Year Variant).
  • Then assigned to each Company Code via OB37 (Assign Fiscal Year Variant to Company Code).
  • Each fiscal year can have up to 12 regular periods plus up to 4 special periods (13–16) for year-end adjustments.
  • All company codes under the same Controlling Area must use the same fiscal year variant — this is not a Company-level constraint. Different Company Codes under the same Company can use different fiscal year variants, as long as each Controlling Area remains internally consistent.

5. Chart of Accounts — The GL Account Structure

The Chart of Accounts (COA) is the master list of all GL accounts available in the system. It defines account numbers, names, and account groups. The Company Code is then assigned to use it.

OB13
Define COA
Create the Chart of Accounts

Define the COA key (e.g. INT for International COA), description, maintenance language, and maximum GL account number length (up to 10 digits).

OB62
Assign COA
Assign Chart of Accounts to Company Code

Each Company Code must be assigned to exactly one Operating COA. Without this, no GL accounts can be created or posted to in that company code.

OBD4
Account Groups
Define Account Groups with Number Ranges

Groups all GL accounts into categories (Assets, Liabilities, Revenue, Expenses). Each group controls: which number range accounts fall into and which fields are required/optional in FS00.

OB53
Retained Earnings
Define Retained Earnings Account

Assigns a P&L Statement account type to a Retained Earnings GL account. At year-end (FAGLGVTR), all P&L balances are zeroed and their net transferred to this account automatically.

6. Posting Period Configuration

Posting periods control which periods are open for posting. This is one of the most actively used configurations throughout the year — opening and closing periods every month.

OBBO
Define Variant
Define Posting Period Variant

Creates a variant key (e.g. K4, Z001) that will hold the period open/close settings. Multiple company codes can share one variant.

OB37
Assign Variant
Assign Posting Period Variant to Company Code

Links the variant to a specific company code. The company code then follows whichever periods are open in that variant.

OB52
Open/Close
Open and Close Posting Periods

Used at month-end to open the next period and close the current one. Set per account type: + (all), A (Assets), D (Customers), K (Vendors), M (Materials), S (GL). Periods 13–16 are special adjustment periods for year-end.

7. Document Types and Number Ranges

Every SAP document is classified by a Document Type that determines what account types are allowed, what number it receives, and how it behaves. You configure both in SPRO.

T-Code Activity What you configure
OBA7Define Document TypesAccount types allowed (GL, Vendor, Customer, Asset), reversal behavior, net posting allowed, authorization group
FBN1Define Number RangesFrom-number and To-number per year per document type. SAP auto-increments. External vs Internal numbering.
  • Document Type SA — GL journal entry (FB50, F-02). Posts to GL accounts only. Number range: 10xxxxxxx.
  • Document Type KR — Vendor Invoice (FB60, MIRO). Posts to Vendor + GL. Number range: 19xxxxxxx.
  • Document Type KZ — Vendor Payment (F-53, F110). Posts to Vendor + Bank. Number range: 15xxxxxxx.
  • Document Type DR — Customer Invoice (FB70). Posts to Customer + GL.
  • Document Type DZ — Customer Payment (F-28). Posts to Customer + Bank.
💡 This is why vendor invoices get numbers like 1900001234 and GL entries get 1000001234 — the number range is assigned per document type in FBN1.

8. Field Status Groups — Controlling What Users Must Fill In

Field Status Groups define which fields are Required, Optional, or Suppressed when posting to a GL account. This prevents users from posting without critical information (like cost centers).

OBC4
Field Status
Maintain Field Status Variants and Groups

Define field status groups (e.g. G001 General, G004 Cost Accounts). For each group, set fields as Suppressed / Optional / Required. Then assign the group to GL accounts in FS00.

OBD2
Assign Variant
Assign Field Status Variant to Company Code

Links the field status variant to a company code. Every GL account in that company code will use the groups defined in the variant.

Field Status Conflict — The Most Common Config Error

The posting key (e.g. 40 for GL Debit) also has its own field status. SAP applies the more restrictive of the two. If the posting key says "Required" and the field status group says "Suppressed", SAP cannot resolve it — error: "Field status conflict." Fix: change one of them to Optional in OBC4.

9. Tolerance Groups — Setting Limits on What Users Can Post

Tolerance groups control how much a user is authorized to post and what payment differences are acceptable during clearing. Configured in SPRO and assigned to users via SU01.

GL Account Tolerance OBA0
  • Maximum debit/credit difference allowed when clearing GL accounts
  • If difference is within tolerance, SAP posts it to a pre-defined difference account automatically
Employee Tolerance OBA4
  • Maximum document amount per user group
  • Maximum individual line item amount
  • Maximum cash discount allowed
  • Assigned to users in SU01 via Tolerance Group field
⚠️ If a user has no tolerance group assigned, SAP blocks all postings. Always assign a tolerance group to every FI user during go-live preparation.

10. Vendor and Customer Account Groups

Just like GL accounts have Account Groups, vendors and customers also have account groups that control number ranges and field layout in the vendor/customer master (BP).

Object Config T-Code Controls
Vendor Account GroupsOBD3Number range for vendor master, which fields are required/optional in BP vendor view (e.g. reconciliation account = required)
Customer Account GroupsOBD2Number range for customer master, which fields are required/optional in BP customer view
Number Ranges (Vendors)XKN1Define number ranges for vendor master records and assign to account groups
Number Ranges (Customers)XDN1Define number ranges for customer master records and assign to account groups

11. House Bank Configuration — FI12

A House Bank is the representation of your company's physical bank account inside SAP. Before the payment program (F110) or any manual bank transaction can post, the house bank must be configured. This is done via T-code FI12 in SPRO.

Configuration Steps
  • FI12 — Define House Banks: Navigate to SPRO → Financial Accounting → Bank Accounting → Bank Accounts → Define House Banks. Create a House Bank key (e.g. HSBC), assign it to Company Code BEV1, enter the bank's country, bank key (routing/swift), and bank name
  • Account ID: Under each House Bank you create one or more Account IDs (e.g. CHECK, USD). Each Account ID maps to a specific bank account number and a GL bank account in FS00
  • GL Account linkage: The G/L account for the bank (e.g. 113100 — Bank Main Account, Cash type) is entered in the Account ID. This is how bank postings flow to the general ledger automatically
House Bank Key Structure
  • Company Code: BEV1
  • House Bank ID: HSBC (user-defined key)
  • Account ID: CHECK or EGP
  • Bank Account No: physical account number
  • G/L Account: 113100 (Bank Main GL)
Why it matters in transactions
  • F-48 / F-53: "House Bank" and "Account ID" fields reference this config
  • F110: payment program selects bank accounts via house bank
  • FBCJ: Cash Journal also references a house bank
  • No house bank = payment transactions fail
FI12Define House Banks — create bank key, account IDs, link to GL account
💡 On projects you will often hear "house bank" used loosely to mean both the bank entity and the GL account. Technically FI12 creates the bank master; the GL account is separate (FS00) and just linked here. In BEV1 exercises the house bank is pre-configured — you reference it in F-48 and F-53 using the bank data fields.

L8 Configuration T-Code Summary

All the key configuration T-codes you need to know from this lecture:

SPROOpen the IMG / configuration cockpit
OX15Define Company (holding entity)
OX02Define Company Code
OX16Assign Company Code to Company
OB29Maintain Fiscal Year Variant
OB37Assign Fiscal Year Variant to Company Code
OB13Define Chart of Accounts
OB62Assign COA to Company Code
OBD4Define GL Account Groups
OB53Define Retained Earnings Account
OBBODefine Posting Period Variant
OB52Open and Close Posting Periods
OBA7Define Document Types
FBN1Define Document Number Ranges
OBC4Maintain Field Status Groups
OBD2Assign Field Status Variant to Co. Code
OBA4Tolerance Groups for Employees
OBA0Tolerance Groups for GL Accounts
OBD3Vendor Account Groups
FI12Define House Banks — bank accounts & GL linkage
SE09Transport Organizer (manage transports)
T-Codes
General & Navigation
FIORI Upload General Journal Entries
SU01 User Maintenance
GL — General Ledger
FS00 Create G/L Account Master Data
FB50 Post G/L Document
F-02 Classic G/L Posting
FB03 Display & Analyze a Document
FB08 Reverse a Posted Document
FBV0 Find & Complete a Parked Document
FBL3N Display GL Account Line Items
FAGLB03 GL Account Period Balances
FBCJ Cash Journal
AP — Accounts Payable
BP Create Vendor (Business Partner)
FB60 Enter Vendor Invoice
F-48 Post Vendor Down Payment
FBKP Special G/L Configuration
F-47 Down Payment Request
F-53 Post Outgoing Payment
F-54 Clear Vendor Down Payment
FBL1N Vendor Line Item Display
FK10N Vendor Balance Display
IDCNAP AP Aging Report
F110 Automatic Payment Run
MIRO Invoice Verification (3-Way Match)
AR — Accounts Receivable
FB70 Enter Customer Invoice
FBL5N Customer Line Item Display
FD10N Customer Balance Display
IDCNAR AR Aging Report
F-28 Post Incoming Payment
F-29 Post Customer Down Payment
F-39 Clear Customer Down Payment
MODULE: Loading...
FIORI

Upload General Journal Entries

Intermediate
📋 Scenario
Post multiple journal entries at once using SAP Fiori's Upload General Journal Entries app. Download the template, fill in entries for Company Code BEV1, upload, and post — all without using the SAP GUI.
1
Open the Fiori App
Log into the SAP Fiori Launchpad. In the search bar, type "Upload General Journal Entries" and open the app. The main screen shows a Browse button to upload a file, and below it a list of previous uploads (Temporary ID, Batch ID, Status, Posting Date). At the bottom: Post, Show Log, and Download Template buttons.
2
Download the Template
Click Download Template (bottom right). A Template Download dialog appears:
• Template Language: English
• Format: choose Spreadsheet (.xlsx) (recommended)

Click Download. The file JournalEntry_Template.xlsx is saved to your computer.
