Review the Proposal — Payment List
Click Display Proposal (or Edit → Proposal → Display Proposal). The Payment Proposal List opens showing every vendor SAP selected for payment. For each vendor you can see:

• Vendor number and name
• Total payment amount
• Payment method (T = Transfer)
• House bank to be used
• Individual invoices included (click the vendor to expand the line items)

This is your chance to review the proposal before anything is posted.
💡 This is the most important review step. Check: are the amounts reasonable? Are any unexpected vendors included? Are there invoices you want to hold back? If anything looks wrong, delete the proposal (Edit → Proposal → Delete Proposal), fix the issue, and re-run.
6
Review the Exception List
From the Proposal List screen, click Exception List (or Edit → Proposal → Exception List). This shows every invoice that SAP could NOT include in the payment run, along with the reason. Common exception reasons:

• "Vendor has no payment method assigned" — go to BP, open the vendor, check the Payment Transactions tab, assign payment method T
• "No house bank found" — the bank configuration in FBZP is missing or incomplete for this company code / payment method combination
• "Invoice is blocked for payment" — a payment block indicator (R, A, etc.) is set on the invoice; use MRBR or FB02 to remove the block
• "Amount below minimum payment amount" — the invoice is below the minimum threshold configured in FBZP
⚠️ Never skip the Exception List. On a real project, exceptions are the first thing the AP Manager asks about after a payment run. An exception means a vendor did NOT get paid — and that vendor may call the next day.
7
Run the Payment (Post)
Return to the Status screen (F110 main screen). Click Payment Run (or Edit → Payment → Create Payment). Check Start Immediately in the dialog and confirm. SAP runs another background job.

After a few seconds, refresh the Status screen. The status updates to: "Payment run has been carried out." You will also see a summary: number of payment documents created and total amount paid.

What SAP has done in the background:
• Created one payment document per vendor (document type ZP)
• Each payment document: Dr AP Reconciliation Account / Cr Bank GL Account
• The vendor's open invoices are now cleared (status changes from open to cleared in FBL1N)
⚠️ This step is IRREVERSIBLE in the normal sense. Once the payment run posts, the payment documents exist in SAP. To reverse, you would need to use FBRA to reset the clearing and then FB08 to reverse individual payment documents — a painful process. Always review the proposal carefully before this step.
8
Verify the Result — FBL1N & FAGLB03
Verify the payment run posted correctly using two checks:

Check 1 — FBL1N (Vendor Line Items):
Open FBL1N for the vendors that were paid. Select Cleared Items. The invoices that were open before the run now show as cleared — the clearing document number is the F110 payment document. The clearing date matches your posting date.

Check 2 — FAGLB03 (GL Balance):
Open FAGLB03 for the bank GL account (e.g. 122225 BANK). The period that was just posted shows a new credit movement — this is the total cash paid out. The AP Reconciliation account (210100) should show a matching debit (reduction in liability).
✅ The golden reconciliation rule: FBL1N cleared items total = FAGLB03 bank movement for that period. If they don't match, something was posted manually outside F110 and needs investigation.
✅ Result: Payment run completed for Company Code BEV1. All due vendor invoices selected in the proposal are now cleared. Payment documents (type ZP) posted: Dr AP Reconciliation Account 210100 / Cr Bank Account 122225. Verified via FBL1N (cleared items) and FAGLB03 (bank GL movement).
⚠️ Common Mistakes
✕Skipping the Exception List review — unpaid vendors are invisible until they complain. Always check exceptions before closing the run.
✕Wrong "Next Payment Date" — if set too far in the future, F110 pulls invoices that aren't due yet and you overpay. If too close, due invoices are skipped.
✕Payment method mismatch — the method entered in F110 Parameters (e.g. T) must match the method assigned in the vendor master in BP. No match = no payment, it goes to exceptions.
✕Running the Payment step before reviewing the proposal — once posted, reversal requires multiple manual steps per document. There is no single "undo" for F110.
🔗 Related T-Codes
FBZPConfigure payment methods, house banks, and bank accounts — must be set up before F110 can run
MRBRRelease blocked invoices before the payment run so they are included
FBL1NVerify vendor invoices are cleared after the payment run
FBRAReset clearing if a payment document needs to be reversed
F-53Manual outgoing payment — used for one-off payments outside the payment run
MIRO

Invoice Verification — 3-Way Match

Advanced
📋 Scenario
Your company (BEV1) raised Purchase Order 4500000123 to vendor 580024 for 100 units of raw material at 50 EGP each = 5,000 EGP. The warehouse received the goods and posted a Goods Receipt. The vendor has now sent their invoice for 5,100 EGP. Use MIRO to enter and verify the invoice — observe how SAP performs the 3-way match and how it handles the price variance.
🔍 What is this T-Code?

MIRO (Logistic Invoice Verification) is how vendor invoices are entered when they are linked to a Purchase Order. SAP automatically compares the invoice price and quantity against the PO and the Goods Receipt — this is the 3-way match. It is the MM-FI integration point: MIRO triggers FI postings without the accountant having to enter any GL accounts manually.

💼 Real-Life Use Case

The warehouse received 100 bags of sugar against PO 4500000123. The vendor invoice arrives at 5,100 EGP instead of the 5,000 EGP on the PO. The AP clerk enters the invoice in MIRO — SAP posts it, calculates a 100 EGP price variance, and automatically blocks the invoice for payment because the 2% variance exceeds the tolerance. AP must get procurement to approve before releasing.

⚡ The 3-Way Match — What SAP is Checking
LEG 1
📋
Purchase Order
What was ordered and at what price? (ME21N)
LEG 2
📦
Goods Receipt
What was actually received? (MIGO) — creates GR/IR clearing entry
LEG 3
🧾
Vendor Invoice
What does the vendor say we owe? (MIRO)
SAP compares all three. If PO qty = GR qty = Invoice qty AND PO price ≈ Invoice price (within tolerance) → invoice posts cleanly and is free for payment. Any variance outside tolerance → invoice posts but is blocked for payment automatically.
📊 The Accounting Entries — What MIRO Posts
STEP 1 — Goods Receipt (MIGO, done before MIRO):
Dr GR/IR Clearing Account (e.g. 191100)  5,000 EGP
Cr Inventory Account (e.g. 300100)            5,000 EGP
The GR/IR account acts as a bridge — it records that goods arrived before the invoice.
STEP 2 — Invoice Receipt (MIRO, this exercise):
Dr GR/IR Clearing Account (191100)   5,000 EGP  ← clears the GR entry
Dr Price Variance Account (e.g. 281000)    100 EGP   ← the 2% overage
Cr AP Reconciliation Account (210100) 5,100 EGP  ← vendor now owed 5,100
The GR/IR account nets to zero (debited in GR, credited in MIRO). Only the variance hits the P&L.
1
Open MIRO — Select Transaction Type
Type MIRO in the command field and press Enter. The Enter Incoming Invoice screen opens.

At the top left, the Transaction dropdown is set to Invoice by default — leave it as Invoice. Other options include Credit Memo, Subsequent Debit, Subsequent Credit — these are used for corrections after the original invoice is posted.

Set the Invoice Date to today: 06.06.2026
Set the Posting Date to today: 06.06.2026
Set the Reference to the vendor's invoice number: INV-2026-0089 (this is the vendor's document number for duplicate detection)
📌 The Reference field is critical — SAP uses it together with vendor number, company code, currency, amount, and date to detect duplicate invoices. Always fill it with the vendor's own invoice number. If left blank, duplicate detection does not work reliably.
2
Enter the PO Number — SAP Pulls Everything
In the PO Reference tab (bottom half of the screen), find the Purchase Order/Scheduling Agreement field. Enter the PO number: 4500000123 and press Enter.

SAP immediately populates the entire invoice line section automatically:
• Vendor number (580024) and name
• PO line items — material, quantity ordered (100), quantity already received (100), unit price (50.00), total (5,000 EGP)
• Quantity to be invoiced is pre-filled at 100 (matching the GR)
• The GR/IR clearing account (191100) is proposed automatically

You do not need to enter any GL accounts — MIRO derives them from the PO and the material master configuration.
💡 This is the key difference between MIRO and FB60. In FB60 you manually enter the GL expense account. In MIRO, SAP derives all accounts automatically from the PO — the GR/IR account, the stock account, and the vendor reconciliation account. The accountant only needs to verify, not specify accounts.
3
Enter the Invoice Amount — Observe the Variance
At the top of the screen, in the Amount field, enter the vendor's invoice amount: 5100. Set Currency: EGP.

As soon as you enter 5,100, look at the balance indicator at the bottom of the screen. SAP shows:

• Invoice amount: 5,100.00
• PO/GR value: 5,000.00
• Difference: 100.00 (highlighted in yellow/orange)

The traffic light in the bottom-left corner turns yellow — this means MIRO can still post, but there is a variance that exceeds zero. If the variance exceeded the configured tolerance limit (e.g. 5%), the light turns red and posting is blocked entirely.
💡 The balance must show 0.00 for a clean match. A yellow light means variance within tolerance (will post but may block for payment). A red light means variance exceeds tolerance and you cannot post at all until either the amount is corrected or the PO is amended in ME22N.
4
Check the Tax and Payment Terms
Review two important fields before posting:

Tax Code: Check the tax code field on the invoice line. SAP proposes the tax code from the PO (e.g. V1 = 14% VAT input tax). If the vendor invoice shows a different tax treatment, adjust here. The tax amount calculates automatically and adds to the total.

Payment Terms / Baseline Date: Click the Details tab at the top of the MIRO screen. Here you see:
• Payment terms (e.g. NT30 = net 30 days) — pulled from the vendor master
• Baseline Date — the date from which the payment due date is calculated (defaults to Invoice Date)
• This determines when F110 will pick up this invoice for payment
📌 Always verify the baseline date. If the vendor wants payment from delivery date (not invoice date), adjust it here. Getting this wrong means either paying too early (losing cash flow) or paying too late (relationship damage or late payment penalties).
5
Simulate Before Posting
Before posting, click Simulate (the document icon in the toolbar, or go to Document → Simulate). SAP shows the proposed accounting document without posting it:

Line 1: Dr GR/IR Clearing (191100)    5,000.00 EGP
Line 2: Dr Price Variance (281000)       100.00 EGP
Line 3: Cr Vendor / AP Recon (210100)  5,100.00 EGP

Verify this matches what you expect. The GR/IR line clears the open goods receipt. The price variance line captures the 100 EGP difference to the P&L. The vendor line is the total liability.
✅ Simulate is your safety check — just like in FB50. Never skip it on MIRO, especially when a variance is showing. Confirm the correct accounts are hit before the document is permanent.
6
Post the Invoice — Read the Result
Click Post (Ctrl+S or the save icon). SAP posts the document and shows a message at the bottom of the screen:

"Document 5105600089 posted"

Note the document number — this is a KR-type document (Vendor Invoice). SAP also shows a secondary message:

"Invoice is blocked for payment: Price variance"

This confirms: the invoice posted successfully and the vendor liability (5,100 EGP) is on the books, but it will NOT appear in F110's payment proposal until the block is released. The payment block is set automatically by SAP because the 2% price variance (100/5,000) exceeded the tolerance configured in OMR6.
⚠️ "Document posted" and "invoice is free for payment" are NOT the same thing. A blocked invoice IS in the books — it updates FBL1N and FAGLB03 — but F110 will skip it. This is one of the most common sources of confusion for new AP consultants.
7
Verify in FBL1N — Check the Payment Block
Open FBL1N. Enter vendor 580024, Company Code BEV1. Select Open Items and execute.

Find the invoice you just posted (document 5105600089, amount 5,100 EGP, document type KR). Look at the Pmnt Block column. It shows R — the "R" indicator means the invoice is blocked for payment due to a variance (set automatically by MIRO).

To release the block: use T-code MRBR (Release Blocked Invoices). In MRBR you can see all blocked invoices with reason codes, compare the variance amounts, and manually release individual invoices after obtaining business approval. Alternatively, if procurement agrees to the price, they can revise the PO in ME22N — SAP will automatically re-check the tolerance and release the block if the variance is now within limits.
💡 The "R" payment block is SAP's automatic control. It is not a mistake — it is the system enforcing your company's purchase price discipline. The AP consultant's job is to manage the exception, not bypass the control. Always involve procurement before releasing an R-blocked invoice.
8
Verify the GR/IR Account — FAGLB03
Open FAGLB03 for account 191100 (GR/IR Clearing Account), Company Code BEV1, current fiscal year.

The GR/IR account should show a net balance of zero for this PO's line — the debit from the Goods Receipt (MIGO, 5,000 EGP) and the credit from the Invoice Receipt (MIRO, 5,000 EGP from the GR/IR line) cancel out.

If the GR/IR account shows a remaining balance, it means either:
• Goods were received but invoice not yet entered (debit balance — normal, wait for invoice)
• Invoice was entered but goods receipt is missing or short (credit balance — problem, investigate)

This reconciliation — checking that GR/IR nets to zero at period end — is a key AP consultant month-end task.
✅ GR/IR reconciliation is one of the top 3 items on every AP month-end checklist. A non-zero GR/IR balance at period end means either an invoice is waiting for a GR or a GR is waiting for an invoice — both need follow-up before the books close.
✅ Result: Vendor invoice for PO 4500000123 entered via MIRO. 3-way match performed by SAP: PO (5,000) vs GR (5,000) vs Invoice (5,100) — 100 EGP price variance detected. Document KR posted: Dr GR/IR 5,000 / Dr Price Variance 100 / Cr AP Reconciliation 210100 5,100. Invoice blocked for payment (R indicator) due to variance exceeding tolerance. GR/IR account nets to zero. Block requires MRBR release or ME22N PO revision before F110 will pay.
⚠️ Common Mistakes
✕Using FB60 for a PO-based invoice — FB60 bypasses the 3-way match entirely, skips GR/IR clearing, and posts directly to an expense account. Always use MIRO when a Purchase Order exists.
✕Forgetting the Reference field — the vendor's invoice number must be entered in Reference. Without it, SAP's duplicate invoice check cannot work and the same invoice can be posted twice.
✕Confusing "posted" with "payable" — a blocked invoice (R indicator) is on the books but will not be paid by F110. Always check FBL1N for the payment block indicator after MIRO.
✕Entering MIRO before the Goods Receipt exists — MIRO requires at least one GR line to match against. If the warehouse hasn't posted the GR (MIGO), MIRO will show the PO quantity but nothing to match, and the invoice will block.
🔗 Related T-Codes
ME21NCreate the Purchase Order that MIRO references
MIGOPost the Goods Receipt (must exist before MIRO)
ME22NAmend the PO price to match the invoice and auto-release the block
MRBRRelease blocked invoices — review exceptions and manually approve payment
OMR6Configure the 3-way match price/quantity tolerances
F110Automatic payment run — will pay this invoice once the R block is released
Lectures
L1Introduction to SAP
L2SAP Landscape & Implementation Methodology
L3GL Account Master Data
L4GL Posting & Document Entry
L5Document Management & Reporting
L6AP — Accounts Payable
L7AP Down Payments & AR
L8SAP FI Configuration
TCT-Code Challenge
Overall Score
0 / 90 correct
0%
L1

Introduction to SAP

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 1

1. What does ERP stand for?
ERP = Enterprise Resource Planning — a system that integrates all core business processes (finance, procurement, HR, sales) into one unified platform with shared data.
2. In what year was SAP founded, and in which country?
SAP was founded in 1972 in Walldorf, Germany by five former IBM engineers. SAP stands for Systems, Applications & Products in Data Processing.
3. What percentage of Fortune 500 companies use SAP?
SAP runs in 77% of Fortune 500 companies, making it the dominant ERP platform globally with over 440,000 customers in more than 180 countries.
4. What is the role of a Key User in an SAP project?
Key Users are experienced client employees who know both the business process and SAP. They train colleagues, handle basic issues, and communicate change requests to the consultant team.
5. Which SAP version introduced the client-server architecture and became a global success?
SAP R/3 (1992) introduced the three-tier client-server architecture and became SAP's first global success. R/1 (1972) covered only finance, R/2 (1979) was mainframe-based.
6. What does the abbreviation "FICO" stand for in SAP?
FICO = Financial Accounting (FI) + Controlling (CO). FI handles external financial reporting; CO handles internal management accounting. Together they form the financial backbone of every SAP implementation.
7. Which SAP product is designed for small businesses with up to approximately 100 employees?
SAP Business One targets small businesses (up to ~100 employees). SAP Business ByDesign targets mid-size companies. SAP S/4HANA and ECC target large enterprises.
8. In an SAP project, who is responsible for the day-to-day operation of the system after go-live?
After go-live, the customer's internal team manages daily operations. The consulting partner provides hyper-care support for 4–8 weeks, then transitions to a support contract or the customer's own support team.
0/8

L2

SAP Landscape & Implementation Methodology

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 2

1. What is the correct order of the SAP System Landscape for transporting changes?
Changes always flow: DEV (Development) → QAS (Quality Assurance / Testing) → PRD (Production). You never make direct changes in the live PRD system.
2. In SAP Activate methodology, during which phase is the Blueprint document created?
The Blueprint is created during the Explore phase (Phase 3). Consultants hold workshops with the client to gather all requirements and document them. The client must sign off before the Realize phase begins.
3. What does WRICEF stand for in SAP projects?
WRICEF = Workflow, Reports, Interfaces, Conversion, Enhancements, Forms. These are all custom development objects built during the Realize phase to extend standard SAP functionality.
4. What is the purpose of the Controlling Area in SAP organizational structure?
The Controlling Area is the highest organizational unit in CO. It can contain one or multiple Company Codes and is the umbrella for all cost centers, profit centers, and internal orders.
5. What is the difference between FI and CO in terms of reporting purpose?
FI produces external reports for shareholders, tax authorities, and regulators (balance sheet, P&L). CO produces internal reports for management decisions — cost centers, profit centers, budgets vs actuals.
6. In SAP Activate, what is the main deliverable of the Prepare phase?
The Prepare phase delivers: project charter, team assignments, system landscape setup (DEV/QAS/PRD provisioning), and initial project planning. The Blueprint is created in the Explore phase.
7. What is a Transport Request in SAP?
A Transport Request (TR) packages all configuration changes made in DEV and moves them through the landscape: DEV → QAS → PRD. Without releasing a TR, changes stay in DEV and never reach production.
8. Which organizational unit in SAP represents a legal entity that produces its own financial statements?
The Company Code is the central FI organizational unit — it represents an independent legal entity with its own balance sheet and P&L. A Client can have multiple Company Codes (e.g. one per country).
0/8

L3

GL Account Master Data

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 3

1. Which Chart of Accounts type is used for all daily postings and is mandatory for every Company Code?
The Operating CoA is mandatory — it is the main chart used for all daily postings. Country CoA maps to local legal requirements (optional). Group CoA is for consolidated reporting (optional).
2. In SAP's GL account number ranges, which range is typically used for Revenue accounts?
In a standard SAP CoA: 100000–199999 = Assets, 200000–299999 = Liabilities, 300000–399999 = Equity, 400000–499999 = Revenue / P&L accounts.
3. What happens when a GL account is set as a Reconciliation Account?
A Reconciliation Account is linked to a sub-ledger (AR, AP, AA). Direct manual journal entries are blocked. You must post via the sub-ledger transaction (e.g. FB60 for vendors), and SAP automatically updates the Recon. Account.
4. What is the T-Code used to create or edit a GL Account Master Data?
FS00 is the main transaction for GL Account Master Data — Create, Change, and Display. FB50 is for posting journal entries. SPRO is for configuration. FB03 is for displaying documents.
5. In the GL Account Master Data, what does the Field Status Group control?
The Field Status Group defines the field layout during posting. For example, G001 (General) makes certain fields like Text and Cost Center optional or mandatory. This controls what the accountant must fill in when posting to this GL account.
6. In FS00, what does setting "Account Currency" to the company code currency mean?
Setting Account Currency = Company Code currency (e.g. EGP) means only postings in that currency are allowed. Set it to a foreign currency (e.g. USD) to accept only USD postings. Leave it blank to allow all currencies.
7. What is the purpose of the Account Group in GL Account Master Data?
The Account Group (e.g. Fixed Asset, Revenue, Liability) controls: (1) the number range for the account, (2) which fields are displayed, required, or hidden in FS00. Every GL account must belong to an Account Group.
8. You are creating a vendor reconciliation GL account in FS00. Which Reconciliation Account type do you select?
Setting Reconciliation Account type to K (Vendors) links the GL account to the AP sub-ledger. All vendor postings (FB60, payments) automatically update this account. Direct manual posting to it is blocked by design.
9. What happens to P&L account balances when you run FAGLGVTR at year-end?
FAGLGVTR closes P&L accounts: their net balance is transferred to the Retained Earnings GL account, and they start the new year at zero. Balance Sheet accounts (Assets, Liabilities, Equity) carry their closing balance forward as the new opening balance.
10. In FS00, what is the difference between "Short Text" and "Long Text"?
Short Text (max 20 characters) appears in dropdown searches, line item displays, and compact reports. Long Text is the full descriptive name used in financial statements and printouts. Both should be meaningful and consistent.
0/10

L4

GL Posting & Document Entry

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 4

1. What is the Posting Key used for a GL Credit entry (e.g. crediting a revenue account)?
Posting Key 50 = GL Account Credit entry. Posting Key 40 = GL Account Debit entry. PstKy 31 = Vendor Invoice (Credit). PstKy 01 = Customer Invoice (Debit).
2. What is the difference between Hold and Park in FB50?
Hold = private draft, only retrievable by the same user. Park = shared draft, visible and editable by other authorized users. Neither updates GL balances — only Post creates the final accounting entry.
3. When creating a GL account in FS00, you activate both "Post Automatically Only" and "Reconciliation Account". What happens?
Both settings block direct manual posting, making them redundant and contradictory. Fix: if you want a Recon. Account, remove "Post Auto Only". If you want system-only posting (e.g. clearing account), use only "Post Auto Only".
4. Which T-Code allows you to copy an existing GL Account Master Data to create a new account?
FS15 = Copy GL Account Master Data. It copies all settings from an existing account to a new account number, saving time and ensuring consistency. FS00 is for creating/editing from scratch.
5. What Posting Key would you use when posting a Vendor Invoice line item?
PstKy 31 = Vendor Credit Invoice. When posting a vendor invoice, the vendor account is credited (liability increases). PstKy 01 = Customer Debit Invoice. PstKy 40/50 = GL Debit/Credit entries.
6. In FB50, what is the key difference between "Document Date" and "Posting Date"?
Document Date = date on the physical document (e.g. invoice date: 28 April). Posting Date = determines which fiscal period the entry hits (e.g. 30 April = Period 4). They can differ — a March invoice can be posted in April if the period is still open.
7. A vendor invoice was posted with the wrong amount and needs to be corrected. What is the correct SAP process?
Posted documents cannot be edited or deleted in SAP — this is a core audit principle. Correct process: (1) Reverse the wrong document with FB08 (creates a mirror-image reversal entry), (2) Post a new correct document. This leaves a complete, tamper-proof audit trail.
8. What does Posting Key 31 tell SAP when entered in F-02?
PstKy 31 = Vendor / Credit. When you enter 31 as the posting key in F-02, SAP knows: (1) this line posts to a Vendor account, (2) it is a Credit entry (increasing the liability). This is the standard posting key for a vendor invoice credit line.
9. When would you use "Park" instead of posting directly in FB50?
Park = shared draft that does NOT update GL balances. It enters an approval workflow — another authorized user (e.g. supervisor) can open it in FBV0, review it, and post it. Use Park whenever a document requires dual control before posting.
10. What controls the automatic assignment of a document number when you post in FB50?
Each Document Type (SA, KR, DR, etc.) is assigned a Number Range in FBN1. SAP automatically picks the next available number from that range when you post. This is why GL documents get 10xxxxxxx numbers and vendor invoices get 19xxxxxxx numbers.
0/10

L5

Document Management & Reporting

📝 Lecture 5 Quiz 5 Questions

1. You are working in T-code FB01 and want to open F-03 without closing your current screen. What do you type in the command field?
/o + T-code opens the transaction in a new session window without closing the current one. /n + T-code would close FB01 first. /nex exits SAP entirely. Typing the T-code alone replaces the current screen.
2. In FB03 Document List, the standard search screen does not show a User Name field. How do you add it?
The Dynamic Selection button opens an additional panel on the left side of the screen with extra filterable fields — including User Name, Transaction Code, Changed On, and more. This allows searching by user or other fields not shown by default.
3. A user posted a wrong journal entry in SAP. What is the correct way to fix it?
SAP never allows deletion of posted documents. The correct approach is to use FB08 (Reverse Document) — enter the document number and a reversal reason, which creates a mirror-image entry that cancels the original. Then post a new, correct document.
4. What does FAGLB03 display, and how is it different from FBL3N?
FAGLB03 shows the GL account balance aggregated by fiscal period — Debit, Credit, Balance, and Cumulative Balance for each period. FBL3N shows individual transaction line items (every document that hit the account). Use FAGLB03 for a monthly summary, FBL3N for a detailed transaction list.
5. What is the correct process for uploading multiple journal entries at once using SAP Fiori?
The correct process in Fiori: search for "Upload General Journal Entries" → click Download Template (choose .xlsx) → fill in header and line item data in the template → click Browse to upload the file → click Post. Use Show Log to verify results and catch any errors.
6. In FB08, what is the impact of using a prior-period reversal reason compared to Reason 01?
Reversal Reason 01 posts the reversal with today's date (current period). Prior-period reasons (e.g. 02) allow you to set a Reversal Date in the original period — so the correction appears in the month the error occurred, not in the current month. Important for accurate monthly P&L.
7. You want to see all GL postings on account 500005 for Q1 2026 including both paid and unpaid items. Which T-code and selection do you use?
FBL3N = GL Account Line Item Display. Select "All Items" to include both open and cleared items. Use the Posting Date range to filter Q1. FAGLB03 shows period totals (summary), not individual line items.
8. What is the primary purpose of T-code FAGLB03?
FAGLB03 = GL Account Balance Display. It shows, per accounting period: opening balance, total debits, total credits, and closing balance. It is the standard tool for reconciliation, trend analysis, and verifying that a GL account balance is correct at month-end.
0/8

L6

AP — Accounts Payable

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 6

1. In SAP S/4HANA, what transaction is used to create a new vendor (Business Partner)?
In SAP S/4HANA, BP (Maintain Business Partner) is the unified T-code for creating vendors, customers, and general business partners. The older transactions XK01/FK01 (for vendors) and XD01/FD01 (for customers) still work but BP is the S/4HANA standard.
2. What is the FI Supplier role code used when adding vendor accounting data in the BP transaction?
FLVN00 Supplier (Fin.Accounting) is the FI role — it unlocks the Company Code tab and Vendor: Account Management tab where you set the Reconciliation Account (210100). FLVN01 Supplier (New) is the Purchasing role where you add the Purchasing Organization data.
3. What document type does SAP assign when you post a vendor invoice via FB60?
FB60 posts a KR document — KR stands for "Kreditor Rechnung" (German for Vendor Invoice). KZ = Vendor Payment. SA = General GL. DR = Customer Invoice. The document type controls number ranges, reversal rules, and what accounts can be posted to.
4. In FBL1N, what does a red circle with a lightning bolt icon (⚡) in the DD column indicate?
In FBL1N, the ⚡ lightning bolt in the DD (Due Date) column means the item is overdue — the net due date has passed and the invoice has not been paid. A plain red circle (no lightning) means open but not yet overdue. A green square means cleared/paid.
5. What is the purpose of the IDCNAP report and what key parameter defines its aging buckets?
IDCNAP = AP Aging Report. It shows all open (unpaid) vendor invoices as of a key date, distributed across aging buckets. The aging buckets are configured in Output Control → Due Date Sorted List (default: 10, 20, 30, 60, 90, 180, 360, 720 days). This report is essential for cash flow planning and payment prioritization.
6. What is the role of the Reconciliation Account assigned in the vendor master (BP)?
The Reconciliation Account (e.g. 210100 — AP) in the vendor master is automatically credited every time you post a vendor invoice via FB60. You never enter it manually — SAP pulls it from the vendor master. This links the AP sub-ledger to the GL balance sheet simultaneously.
7. In FBL1N, you want to see only unpaid vendor invoices as of today. Which selection do you choose?
Open Items = invoices not yet paid. Key Date = today means SAP shows all items open as of that date. "Cleared Items" shows already-paid invoices. "All Items" shows both — useful for full history but not filtered to just outstanding payables.
8. A vendor invoice posted in FB60 used the wrong GL expense account and is still unpaid. What is the correct correction process?
Posted documents are immutable in SAP. Correct process: (1) FB08 to reverse the wrong invoice (creates a mirror reversal, cancels the wrong GL posting), (2) Post a new FB60 with the correct GL expense account. This leaves a complete audit trail of the error and the correction.
9. What does a negative cumulative balance in FK10N indicate for a vendor account?
Vendor accounts in SAP AP carry a Credit balance = normal liability (you owe them). A negative cumulative balance in FK10N = your company owes that vendor money. This is the expected state for an active supplier. A positive balance (unusual) would mean the vendor owes you, e.g. from an overpayment.
10. In FBCJ Cash Journal, what is the difference between "Save" and "Post Selected"?
FBCJ is a two-step process: (1) Save = stores the cash entry as a draft in the journal — GL is NOT yet updated. (2) Post Selected = creates the actual Follow-On FI document that posts to all relevant GL accounts. Both steps are required to complete a cash transaction.
0/10

Lecture 7

AP Down Payments & AR — Order-to-Cash

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 7

1. Transaction F-47 creates a down payment request with special G/L indicator F. What is the accounting effect of this posting?
F-47 creates a noted item — it is purely statistical and does not change any G/L balance. It serves as a reminder/trigger that an advance is expected but no real accounting entry is made until F-48 posts the actual down payment.
2. In F-48 (Post Vendor Down Payment), what special G/L indicator must you enter, and why?
Special G/L indicator A directs the advance to the alternative reconciliation account (the down-payment account), separating it from normal vendor invoices on the standard recon account. This ensures advances are reported separately in financial statements.
3. What is the correct sequence for the full AP advance cycle?
The correct sequence is: F-47 (request) → F-48 (post advance) → F-54 (clear advance against invoice) → F-53 (pay the remaining balance). Sequence matters — you cannot clear the advance (F-54) before the invoice arrives (FB60), and you must clear before paying the net balance (F-53).
4. Transaction F-54 "clears the vendor down payment." What does this mean in accounting terms?
F-54 is a transfer posting — it moves the advance from the special G/L (down-payment) account back to the normal vendor reconciliation account, effectively offsetting it against the invoice. Only the net amount remains as an open item. The advance is now "cleared" — it no longer shows separately as a receivable from the vendor.
5. In FBKP, you assign an alternative reconciliation account for special G/L indicator A. What account type do you select for vendor down payments?
In FBKP you choose account type K (Kreditor = Vendor) to configure the vendor down payment special G/L. Account type D would be for customer down payments, G for GL, and A for assets. Getting the account type wrong means the special G/L won't apply to the right sub-ledger.
6. Which FI role code is used in BP to add the customer accounting (AR) data for Company Code BEV1?
FLCU00 is the FI customer role — it unlocks the Company Code data tab where you enter the Reconciliation Account for receivables. FLCU01 is for the SD/Sales area view. FLVN00 is the vendor equivalent. You must add FLCU00 before any AR posting can be made to the customer.
7. FB70 enters a customer invoice. What document type and posting does SAP create?
FB70 creates document type DR (Debitor Rechnung = Customer Invoice). The entry is: Customer account Dr (they owe us) / Revenue account Cr. This is the AR mirror of FB60 (which creates KR for vendor invoices).
8. What T-code is used to run the automated dunning process for overdue customer invoices?
F150 is the dunning run — it identifies overdue customer open items, assigns escalation levels based on the dunning procedure, and generates dunning notices. FBMP is the configuration T-code (where dunning procedures are defined). F110 is the payment program (vendor side). FBL5N is just a reporting transaction.
0/8

Lecture 8

SAP FI Configuration

🧪 Quiz — Lecture 8

1. What is the correct transport path for SAP configuration changes?
Changes always flow DEV → QAS → PRD. Development is done in DEV, tested in QAS (Quality Assurance), and only then transported to PRD (Production). Never configure directly in PRD — this is one of the most important rules in SAP projects.
2. Which T-code defines the fiscal year variant and which one assigns it to a Company Code?
OB29 (Maintain Fiscal Year Variant) is where you create and define the variant (e.g. K4 = Jan–Dec). OB37 (Assign FY Variant to Company Code) links that variant to a specific Company Code. This is a two-step process — defining the variant alone does nothing until it's assigned.
3. A posting fails with "Period not open." What T-code do you use to fix this?
OB52 is used to open and close individual posting periods. If a period is closed, postings with a date in that period will fail. OBBO only creates the variant key; the actual period control lives in OB52. This is one of the most commonly executed month-end config tasks.
4. In SAP Field Status, if the posting key has field status "Required" and the GL account has "Suppressed" for the same field, what happens?
SAP applies the more restrictive of the two field status settings — but Required vs Suppressed is a direct conflict and causes a field status error that prevents posting. The consultant must resolve the conflict by changing one of the settings (usually making the GL account field "Optional" instead of "Suppressed").
5. What is the T-code to define House Banks in SAP FI configuration?
FI12 (Define House Banks) is the configuration T-code where you create the house bank key, assign it to the Company Code, and link it to the physical bank account and the GL bank account. Without FI12 configuration, payment transactions (F-48, F-53, F110) cannot reference a bank account.
6. What does OBD4 configure, and what does it control?
OBD4 defines GL Account Groups — these group GL accounts by category (Assets, Liabilities, Revenue, Expenses etc.) and control the number ranges assigned to each group and which fields are required/optional in the FS00 master data screen. OBD3 is for Vendor Account Groups; OBD2 is for Customer Account Groups.
7. If a user has no Tolerance Group assigned in SU01, what happens when they try to post a document in FI?
If a user has no tolerance group, SAP blocks all FI postings. There is no "default unlimited" behaviour — this is a common go-live mistake where a new user is created but not assigned a tolerance group. Always assign a tolerance group (OBA4) to every FI user.
8. What is the fiscal year variant constraint related to Controlling Areas?
The constraint is at the Controlling Area level — all Company Codes under the same Controlling Area must use the same fiscal year variant. This is because CO allocations and cost flows must align across the same period. It is not a Company-level constraint — different Company Codes under the same holding Company can use different fiscal year variants as long as they belong to different Controlling Areas.
0/8

TC

T-Code Challenge

One question per T-code — 20 questions total

🎯 T-Code Quiz 20 Questions

1. (FB03) — In FB03, which view replaces posting keys with actual GL account numbers?
GL View replaces customer/vendor codes with actual account numbers. S = Soll (Debit) and H = Haben (Credit) in German accounting notation.
2. (FB08) — Reversal Reason "01" in FB08 is used for which type of reversal?
Reason 01 = Reversal in current period — the most common code. Other codes exist specifically for prior-period reversals and different correction scenarios.
3. (FBV0) — What is the key difference between a Parked document and a Held document?
Held = private draft (only the creator can open it). Parked = shared draft accessible to any authorized user and can enter an approval workflow via FBV0.
4. (FBL3N) — When selecting "Open Items" in FBL3N, which field is essential for accurate results?
The Key Date tells SAP which items were still open as of that specific date. Without it, results may be inaccurate or show no data — especially when analysing prior periods.
5. (FAGLB03) — In FAGLB03, Ledger "0L" refers to what?
0L = Leading Ledger — the primary ledger used for official financial statements. Other ledgers (e.g. 2L, 3L) may exist for IFRS or local GAAP parallel accounting.
6. (SU01) — Which T-code reveals exactly which authorization object is missing when a user gets an access error?
SU53 stores the last failed authorization check for a user — showing exactly which object, field, and value caused the error. Far more useful than calling the helpdesk.
7. (FBCJ) — After saving a cash entry in FBCJ, what must you do to update GL balances?
Save stores the entry as a draft. Post Selected creates the Follow-On accounting document that actually updates GL balances. Both steps are required.
8. (FIORI Upload) — In the Fiori GL Journal upload, what happens if one entry in the Excel template is unbalanced?
SAP validates each entry individually. An unbalanced entry (Dr ≠ Cr) is rejected and shown in the error log, while other balanced entries in the same file post successfully.
9. (FS00) — When creating a P-type (P&L) account in FS00, which field in the Control Data tab is mandatory?
CElem Category connects the GL account to SAP CO (Controlling). Without it, postings will fail with a CO error. Values: 1=Primary Costs, 11=Revenue, 12=Sales Deductions.
10. (FB50) — In FB50's D/C column, what does the indicator "S" stand for?
SAP uses German accounting notation. S = Soll (Debit), H = Haben (Credit). This appears in FB50 and in all posted documents displayed via FB03.
11. (F-02) — In F-02, which Posting Key is used for a GL Credit line?
PK 40 = GL Debit, PK 50 = GL Credit. For vendor lines: PK 31 = Vendor Credit (Invoice), PK 25 = Vendor Debit (Payment). Wrong posting key reverses your entry silently.
12. (BP) — In SAP S/4HANA, transaction BP replaces which two legacy T-codes?
XK01 = Create Vendor (ECC), XD01 = Create Customer (ECC). In S/4HANA both are unified under BP — one master record can have Vendor, Customer, and General roles simultaneously.
13. (FB60) — When you post a vendor invoice in FB60, which account is automatically credited by SAP?
FB60 posts: Dr = GL Expense account (what you specify) / Cr = AP Reconciliation Account (pulled automatically from vendor master). This links the vendor sub-ledger to the GL balance sheet.
14. (FBL1N) — In FBL1N results, what does a ⚡ lightning bolt indicator mean?
Lightning bolt = overdue (due date passed, not yet paid). Plain red circle = open but not yet due. Green square = cleared and matched. Key indicators to prioritize payment runs.
15. (FK10N) — In FK10N, vendor 580024 shows a cumulative balance of 9,443−. What does this mean?
In AP, a negative cumulative balance is completely normal — it means your company has a liability to this vendor. Negative = you owe them. Positive = they owe you (unusual, may indicate overpayment).
16. (IDCNAP) — In IDCNAP, where do you configure the aging buckets (10, 20, 30 days etc.)?
Output Control → Due Date Sorted List defines the aging buckets. Default: 10, 20, 30, 60, 90, 180, 360, 720 days. Customize to match your company's payment terms (e.g. Net 30, Net 45).
17. (FB03 / FB08) — After reversing a document with FB08, can the original document still be found in FB03?
SAP never deletes posted documents — this is a fundamental audit principle. Both the original document and the reversal document remain permanently visible in FB03. The original shows "Reversed" status and the reversal document number; the reversal shows the original document number.
18. (FS00 / FBL3N) — Postings to GL account 501999 keep failing with a CO error. What is most likely missing?
P-type (P&L) accounts must have a CElem Category to integrate with SAP CO. Without it, every posting requiring a cost object (cost center, internal order) fails with a CO error. Fix: FS00 → Control Data tab → set CElem Category (1=Primary Costs, 11=Revenue, 12=Sales Deductions).
19. (FBL1N / FK10N) — What is the key operational difference between FBL1N and FK10N?
FBL1N = line item level — every individual document (each invoice, each payment) as a separate row with its own date, amount, and status. FK10N = period summary — total debits, credits, and balance per month. Use FBL1N to investigate specific transactions; FK10N for reconciliation and trend analysis.
20. (FB60 / FBL1N) — After posting a vendor invoice in FB60, where does the automatic offsetting credit post?
FB60 posts: Dr = GL expense account (you enter this) / Cr = AP Reconciliation Account (SAP pulls this automatically from the vendor master — you never type it). This is the core FI/AP integration: the vendor sub-ledger and the GL balance sheet are updated simultaneously in one document.
0/20

CONSULTANT TRACK

Beyond the T-Code

This track bridges the gap between knowing how to use SAP and thinking like a consultant. Each module covers the full business process, configuration awareness, real troubleshooting scenarios, and integration points.

✅ GL Complete
✅ AP Complete
✅ AR Complete
✅ Bank Complete
✅ Assets Complete
✅ Config Complete
Modules
GLGeneral Ledger
APAccounts Payable
ARAccounts Receivable
BNKBank Accounting
AAAsset Accounting
CFGConfiguration
GL CONSULTANT TRACK

General Ledger The Consultant Layer

You can post, display, reverse, and report. Now learn why it works that way and what happens when it breaks.

1. The Real GL Workflow What Accountants Do Every Month

In your course you learned individual T-codes. In a real company, GL is driven by a monthly closing process a sequence of steps that must happen in the right order. Missing one step creates errors that are painful to fix.

The Monthly GL Closing Sequence
1
Complete All Postings for the Period
All invoices, payments, journal entries must be posted before closing begins. Nothing can be added after the period closes.
2
Post Recurring Entries F.14
Automatically posts pre-defined recurring documents (rent, insurance, depreciation accruals). Must run before closing.
3
Post Accruals and Deferrals FBS1 / F.81
Accruals are expenses incurred but not yet invoiced. FBS1 posts them with a reversal date; F.81 auto-reverses them next period.
4
Run Foreign Currency Revaluation FAGL_FC_VAL
Revalues open items in foreign currencies at the current exchange rate. Required by IFRS/GAAP if the company has forex transactions.
5
Reconcile Sub-Ledgers to GL
Verify AP totals (FBL1N totals = GL account 210100 balance), AR totals, Asset values. Any difference means a posting error that must be found.
6
Close the Posting Period OB52
Lock the period so no more postings can go into it. Only done after all reconciliation is complete. Special periods 13-16 remain available for authorized year-end adjustments.
Consultant reality: In most companies this entire sequence must be completed within 3-5 business days of month-end. Knowing the sequence protects you from making expensive errors under time pressure.

2. Posting Period Control The Gate of GL

One of the most common calls an FI consultant gets: "I cannot post a document, it says the posting period is not open." Understanding this means you solve it in 60 seconds.

  • Every Company Code is assigned a Posting Period Variant (e.g. K4, Z001). This variant defines which periods are open.
  • T-code OB52 is where you open and close periods. You set it per Account Type: + (all types), A (Assets), D (Customers), K (Vendors), M (Materials), S (GL accounts).
  • Each row defines a From Period and To Period that are open. Anything outside that range is blocked.
  • Periods 13, 14, 15, 16 are special adjustment periods used only by senior accountants for year-end adjustments after regular period 12 is closed.
What a student knows

When I get a period error I need to ask someone to fix it.

What a consultant knows

Go to OB52, find the correct Posting Period Variant, check if the period is in the From-To range for that account type, open it if authorized.

Real Scenario: Wrong Period Posting

A junior accountant posted an invoice dated January in February because the period was still open. The January P&L is now wrong.

Consultant approach: Step 1 - Identify the document in FB03. Step 2 - If January is still open in OB52, reverse with FB08 and repost with correct January date. Step 3 - If January is already closed, reverse in February then post a period allocation correction entry. Step 4 - Document the correction and notify the CFO.

3. Recurring Entries and Accruals The GL Work Nobody Teaches

In real companies a large portion of GL postings happen automatically through recurring entries and accruals. A consultant who does not know these will be lost on any real project.

Recurring Entry Documents
  • FBD1 Create a Recurring Entry document. Define the GL accounts, amounts, first run date, last run date, and interval (monthly, quarterly). SAP stores this as a template and does NOT post immediately.
  • F.14 Execute Recurring Entries every month-end. SAP finds all templates due and posts them automatically as real accounting documents.
  • Common uses: monthly rent, insurance premiums, depreciation accruals, management fee allocations.
Consultant tip: Always check if F.14 has been run before closing a period. Many month-end errors come from recurring entries not executed.
Accrual Documents
  • FBS1 Post an Accrual document. You post the entry AND set a Reversal Date in the same screen. The document posts normally but is flagged for automatic reversal.
  • F.81 Execute Scheduled Reversals at the start of the next period. SAP automatically reverses all FBS1 documents whose reversal date has passed.
  • Example: Electricity for December not yet billed. Post Dr Electricity Expense Cr Accrued Liabilities on Dec 31 with reversal date Jan 1. F.81 reverses automatically. When real invoice arrives it posts normally.
Real Scenario: Accrual Not Reversed

An accountant posted an accrual in December using FB50 (a regular journal entry, not FBS1). Nobody reversed it in January. The real invoice came in February and was posted. Result: the expense was counted twice. Prevention: Always use FBS1 for accruals. FBS1 forces you to set a reversal date and F.81 handles it automatically.

4. Document Types and Number Ranges Why They Matter

A consultant needs to understand what each document type controls because clients constantly ask to add new ones or change existing ones.

Type Name Posts to T-Code
SAGL DocumentGL accounts onlyFB50, F-02
KRVendor InvoiceVendor + GLFB60, MIRO
KZVendor PaymentVendor + Bank GLF-53, F110
DRCustomer InvoiceCustomer + GLFB70
DZCustomer PaymentCustomer + Bank GLF-28
AAAsset DocumentAsset + GLAsset T-codes
  • Each document type has its own number range configured in FBN1. This is why a vendor invoice gets a 19xxxxxxx number and a GL document gets a 10xxxxxxx number.
  • Document types control which account types can be used. A KR document cannot post to Asset accounts directly.
  • They also control reversal behavior and whether negative posting is allowed.

5. The 6 Configuration Objects Behind GL

Every time you posted a document in FB50, six configuration objects worked silently in the background. When something goes wrong, one of these six is always the cause.

OB13
Chart of Accounts
Defines the GL account structure

Every Company Code must be assigned to a Chart of Accounts. If wrong: GL accounts do not exist and no postings are possible.

OBBO / OB52
Posting Periods
Controls which periods are open for posting

OBBO defines the posting period variant (the container). OB52 is used at month-end to open or close specific periods within that variant. OB37 assigns the variant to a Company Code. If wrong: users cannot post or can post to closed periods (audit risk).

OBA7
Document Types
Defines document type properties

Configures which account types each document type allows, reversal behavior, and required fields. If wrong: users cannot post invoices or documents cannot be reversed.

FBN1
Number Ranges
Assigns number sequences to document types

Each document type gets a number range per year. SAP increments automatically. If wrong: number range exhausted and posting fails, or two types share a range causing document number overlaps.

OBC4
Field Status Groups
Controls which fields appear when posting

Defines for every field whether it is Suppressed, Optional, or Required. The posting key also has a field status and the more restrictive one wins. If wrong: field status conflict error blocks all postings to the account.

OBA4
Tolerance Groups
Controls how much a user can post

Defines maximum document amount, maximum line item amount, and maximum cash discount. If wrong: junior accountant can post a 10 million EGP document by mistake, or senior accountant cannot post a large legitimate invoice.

6. The 5 GL Errors Every Consultant Faces on Day One

These are not theoretical. Every FI consultant encounters these in the first 90 days on a project.

Error 1: Posting period not open

Cause: Period is closed in OB52.
Solution: Go to OB52, find the Posting Period Variant, open the required period, user retries.
Prevention: Agree a period opening/closing calendar with the client at project start.

Error 2: Field status conflict

Cause: Field Status Group on the GL account and the Field Status of the Posting Key conflict. One says Required, the other says Suppressed. SAP cannot resolve it.
Solution: Go to OBC4, find the Field Status Group of the account, align the conflicting field to Optional in both.
Example: Cost Center is Required on the posting key but Suppressed on the account.

Error 3: Not authorized for this amount

Cause: Document amount exceeds the user Tolerance Group limit in OBA4.
Solution: Increase the tolerance for this user group (requires authorization) or have a senior user post it.
Consultant note: Never increase tolerances without written approval from the financial controller. This is an audit control.

Error 4: Cannot post to this account directly

Cause: The user is trying to post directly to a Reconciliation Account (210100 Vendors, 110100 Customers, or an Asset account). Direct posting to reconciliation accounts is blocked by design.
Solution: Use the correct T-code. FB60 for vendor invoices, FB70 for customer invoices. Never try to bypass via F-02 with posting key 40/50 directly to a recon account.

Error 5: Document cannot be reversed

Cause: The document has already been cleared (e.g. an invoice that was paid). Cleared documents cannot be reversed directly.
Solution: Use FBRA (Reset Clearing) to unlink the payment from the invoice, then reverse the original document with FB08, then re-clear or re-pay as needed.
Warning: This affects the vendor/customer account balance. Always verify the impact before doing it in production.

7. GL Integration How Every Module Feeds Into GL

GL is the automatic receiver of every financial event in the entire SAP system. Understanding these flows is what separates a GL consultant from a GL end-user.

MM to GL
  • Goods Receipt: Dr Inventory / Cr GR/IR Clearing
  • Invoice (MIRO): Dr GR/IR / Cr Vendor
  • Goods Issue: Dr COGS / Cr Inventory
SD to GL
  • Billing: Dr Customer / Cr Revenue
  • Credit Memo: reverse of billing
  • Down Payment: Special GL entry
AA to GL
  • Acquisition: Dr Asset / Cr Vendor or Bank
  • Depreciation: Dr Depr. Expense / Cr Accum. Depr.
  • Retirement: Dr Accum. Depr. + Loss / Cr Asset
CO to GL
  • Every GL posting with a cost center creates a parallel CO document
  • Internal Orders and Profit Centers generate reconciliation entries
  • CO-PA settlement posts to revenue and WIP in GL
The consultant rule: When a client says the GL balance for account X is wrong, do not only look at manual FB50 postings. Check all four integration sources above. The error may have originated in MM, SD, AA, or CO, not in GL itself.

8. Year-End GL Closing The Most Critical Process

Two specific T-codes that every GL consultant must know for year-end:

FAGLGVTR Balance Carryforward

Carries the closing balances of Balance Sheet accounts (Assets, Liabilities, Equity) forward to period 0 of the new fiscal year. P&L accounts are zeroed out and their balance is transferred to the Retained Earnings account. This is why in January of a new year, Balance Sheet accounts have an opening balance and P&L accounts start at zero.

FAGLGVTR can be run in simulation mode during the year. The final run must happen before any postings in the new year are reported. Running it late means the new year starts with wrong opening balances.
OB52 Special Periods 13 to 16

After period 12 (December) is closed, auditors and senior accountants may still need to post year-end adjustments. SAP provides special periods 13, 14, 15, and 16 opened separately in OB52 and accessible only to authorized users. This allows year-end audit adjustments without reopening the regular December period.

9. FI-CO Integration — What Really Happens When You Post

Every time a document is posted in FI, SAP does not just update the GL. It simultaneously creates a parallel document in Controlling (CO). Understanding this is non-negotiable for any FI consultant — most real posting errors are actually CO errors in disguise.

The Parallel Document Principle

When you post a vendor expense invoice (FB60 or MIRO), SAP creates at minimum two documents simultaneously from a single posting:

FI Document
Type KR
Dr Expense GL Account (e.g. 400100 Office Supplies) / Cr AP Reconciliation Account (210100). This is the legal accounting entry — it satisfies external reporting requirements (balance sheet, P&L).
CO Document
Cost Element
The same expense amount is posted to a Cost Center (or Internal Order, or WBS Element). This is the internal management accounting entry — it answers "which department or project incurred this cost?" No external impact; purely for internal reporting.
The CElem Category — The Bridge Between FI and CO

For SAP to create a CO document automatically when a GL account is posted, the GL account must be a Primary Cost Element. This is set in FS00 → Control Data tab → CElem Category field. Without it, postings to P&L accounts either fail or create FI documents with no CO counterpart — leaving cost center reports empty.

CElem Category Account Type CO Object Required at Posting Example GL Account
1Primary CostsCost Center or Internal Order400100 Salaries, 410200 Rent
11RevenueProfit Center (via SD) or CO-PA object800100 Product Revenue
12Sales DeductionsCO-PA object820100 Discounts Given
(none)Balance Sheet AccountNo CO object needed210100 AP Recon, 100100 Cash
Cost Centers, Profit Centers, Internal Orders — When Each Is Used
Cost Center
KS01 to create
Represents a department or functional area (e.g. CC-FIN = Finance, CC-IT = IT, CC-SALES = Sales). Collects all costs incurred by that department. Used for cost centre reporting — "how much did Finance spend this month?" Required on all P&L postings where CElem category is 1.
Profit Center
KE51 to create
Represents a business segment (e.g. PC-BEVERAGES, PC-SNACKS). Collects both revenues and costs, enabling a segment P&L. In S/4HANA, profit center is derived automatically from the cost center, material, or account assignment — the user rarely enters it manually. Profit center documents are always created alongside FI documents in S/4HANA (mandatory in the Universal Journal).
Internal Order
KO01 to create
Used for temporary cost collection on a specific activity or project (e.g. marketing campaign, office renovation). Costs accumulate on the order, then at period-end the order is settled (KO88) — distributing the costs to cost centers or capitalized to an asset. Once settled, the order balance returns to zero.
How to Read a CO Error — The Most Common FI Posting Failure
"Account 400100 requires an assignment to a CO object"

Cause: The GL account has CElem Category 1 (Primary Cost) set in FS00 but the user did not enter a Cost Center or Internal Order on the line item.
Fix for the user: Enter a Cost Center in the account assignment field.
Fix if the account should not need CO: The CElem Category is set incorrectly — this account should be a balance sheet account. Correct it in FS00 → Control Data tab → remove the CElem Category.

"Cost Center CC-FIN is not valid for posting in period 6 / fiscal year 2026"

Cause: The cost center has a validity period that does not cover the posting date. Cost centers have from/to dates set during master data creation.
Fix: In KS02 (Change Cost Center), extend the validity period to cover the required date. Requires CO authorization — coordinate with the CO consultant or Basis.
Prevention: When creating cost centers, always set the valid-from date to the start of the fiscal year and leave valid-to open (31.12.9999).

"Internal Order 700001 has status CLSD — posting not permitted"

Cause: The internal order was closed (status set to CLSD) after settlement. No further postings are allowed.
Fix: In KO02 (Change Internal Order), check the status and reopen if the closure was premature. Or use a different, active internal order.
Note: This is intentional in most cases — the FI consultant should verify with the CO team before changing order status.

💡 The FI-CO consultant handshake: On every project, the FI consultant and the CO consultant must agree on the account assignment strategy during the Blueprint — which accounts require cost centers, which use internal orders, and how profit centers are derived. A mismatch between FI field status (Cost Center = Required in OBC4) and CO master data (cost center not yet created) is the most common source of integration errors in the first week after go-live.

10. Parallel Accounting — When One Set of Books Is Not Enough

Most SAP training courses never mention this topic. Yet on any multinational client, any listed company, or any client subject to both local tax rules and international reporting standards, parallel accounting is a day-one requirement. Understanding it separates a junior consultant from someone who can scope a project correctly.

Why Companies Need More Than One Set of Books

Different accounting standards treat the same business events differently. A company may be legally required to report under two or more standards simultaneously. The most common scenario:

IFRS (International)
  • Required for stock exchange listing (LSE, NYSE, etc.)
  • Assets depreciated using component approach
  • Leases on balance sheet (IFRS 16)
  • Revenue recognition under IFRS 15
  • Used for investor and group reporting
Local GAAP (e.g. Egyptian GAAP)
  • Required by local tax authority and regulators
  • Different depreciation rates and methods
  • Different treatment of provisions and accruals
  • Tax-driven valuations may differ from IFRS
  • Used for statutory filing and tax return
⚠️ A company cannot just file two different Excel spreadsheets. Both sets of books must be maintained in the live SAP system simultaneously — with full audit trail, period closing, and financial statements for each standard. This is what parallel accounting solves.
How SAP Implements Parallel Accounting — Ledgers

In S/4HANA, parallel accounting is implemented through the Ledger concept in the Universal Journal (table ACDOCA). Every posting is stored with a ledger assignment. The standard setup:

0L
Leading Ledger
The main ledger. Every single posting in SAP — every FB50, FB60, MIRO, F110 — automatically posts to 0L. It is the default and is always active. Financial statements from FAGLB03 and standard FI reports always reference 0L unless you specify otherwise. Typically configured for IFRS or the primary reporting standard.
2L / 3L
Non-Leading Ledger
Additional ledgers for other standards. A non-leading ledger (e.g. 2L for Local GAAP) receives all postings from 0L automatically by default. Only differences — where the two standards diverge — require separate postings made exclusively to 2L. This means the 98% of postings that are identical under both standards are only entered once.
EL
Extension Ledger
S/4HANA innovation — a delta ledger. The Extension Ledger does not store its own copy of all postings. Instead it stores only the differences from its reference ledger (0L). At reporting time, SAP combines 0L + Extension Ledger on the fly. This dramatically reduces data volume and is the recommended approach for new S/4HANA implementations. Used for management reporting adjustments, group eliminations, or what-if scenarios.
A Practical Example — Depreciation Difference
Asset: Delivery Van purchased for 120,000 EGP
IFRS (Leading Ledger 0L)
Useful life: 10 years
Annual depreciation: 12,000 EGP
Dr Depr. Expense 12,000 / Cr Accum. Depr. 12,000
Local GAAP (Non-Leading Ledger 2L)
Useful life: 5 years (tax authority rule)
Annual depreciation: 24,000 EGP
Only the 12,000 EGP difference is posted to 2L exclusively
Result: The IFRS balance sheet (0L) shows accumulated depreciation of 12,000. The Local GAAP balance sheet (0L + 2L combined) shows 24,000. Both are correct under their respective standards. Only one extra journal entry was needed.
What the Consultant Configures — FINSC_LEDGER
1
Define Ledgers — SPRO → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Ledgers → Ledger → Define Ledgers for General Ledger Accounting. Create 0L (Leading), 2L (Non-Leading for Local GAAP). Each ledger has a currency, accounting principle, and fiscal year variant.
2
Assign Accounting Principles — Link each ledger to an accounting principle (IFRS, LOCAL). This controls which valuation rules and depreciation areas apply when SAP posts automatically.
3
Define Ledger Groups — A Ledger Group bundles ledgers together so you can post to multiple ledgers in one transaction. The default group includes all ledgers. You can create a group containing only 2L for postings exclusive to Local GAAP.
4
Asset Accounting Depreciation Areas — In Asset Accounting (AA), each depreciation area maps to a ledger. Area 01 → 0L (IFRS depreciation). Area 30 → 2L (Local GAAP depreciation). SAP automatically posts different depreciation amounts to each ledger based on the area configuration. This is configured in SPRO → Asset Accounting → Depreciation → Depreciation Areas.
Impact on Your Scope as an FI Consultant
Single Ledger Client
  • Only 0L configured
  • One fiscal year, one currency
  • Standard FI config — simpler scope
  • All standard reports work out of the box
  • Typical for small/medium local companies
Parallel Ledger Client
  • 0L + 2L (or more) configured
  • Separate closing processes per ledger
  • All financial statements must be run per-ledger
  • AA depreciation areas must be aligned to ledgers
  • FAGLB03 and reports need ledger selection
  • Typical for listed or multinational companies
⚠️ Blueprint question that catches junior consultants: The client asks — "Do we need parallel ledgers?" The wrong answer is to say no without asking. The right answer is to ask: Are you listed on a stock exchange? Do you file under both IFRS and local GAAP? Do you have foreign subsidiaries with different fiscal years? If any answer is yes, parallel ledgers are likely in scope and the configuration complexity doubles. Scope this wrong in the Blueprint and you will be rebuilding the entire ledger setup mid-project.

GL Consultant T-Code Reference

These are the T-codes you will not find in a basic course but will use on every project:

OB52Open and close posting periods
FBD1Create recurring entry document
F.14Execute recurring entries at month-end
FBS1Post accrual or deferral document
F.81Execute scheduled reversals
FBRAReset clearing before reversal
FAGLGVTRYear-end balance carryforward
FAGL_FC_VALForeign currency revaluation
OBA7Configure document types
FBN1Maintain document number ranges
OBC4Field status groups
OBA4Tolerance groups for users
AP CONSULTANT TRACK

Accounts Payable — The Consultant Layer

You can post invoices, run vendor reports, and process payments. Now learn how AP is designed, configured, and kept under control on a live project.

1. The AP Business Process — End to End

In real companies, AP is a controlled purchase-to-pay cycle from the moment a purchase order is raised to the moment the vendor is paid. A consultant must understand every step and where it can break.

The Purchase-to-Pay (P2P) Cycle
1
Purchase Order — MM ME21N
Procurement creates a PO. This is a legal commitment to pay a vendor. No FI posting yet — purely a commitment document stored in MM.
2
Goods Receipt — MM MIGO
Warehouse confirms goods arrived. SAP auto-posts: Dr Inventory / Cr GR/IR Clearing. This is when the liability becomes real in FI.
3
Invoice Receipt — FI MIRO / FB60
MIRO for PO-based invoices (3-way match: PO + GR + Invoice). FB60 for non-PO invoices (rent, utilities, consulting). Posting: Dr GR/IR or Expense / Cr Vendor (Reconciliation Account 210100).
4
Invoice Approval (Workflow)
Most companies require invoice approval before payment. Handled via SAP Workflow or by parking invoices (FBV0) for supervisor posting. Consultant designs and documents the approval matrix during Blueprint.
5
Automatic Payment Run F110
On payment run date, AP runs F110. SAP selects all due invoices, generates payment documents, sends files to the bank. Posting: Dr Vendor / Cr Bank GL.
6
AP Month-End Closing
Reconcile FBL1N totals to GL 210100 balance. Run aging report (IDCNAP). Verify all invoices are posted. Close posting period for Vendors (account type K) in OB52.
💡 Key distinction: MIRO has 3-way match validation built-in (it checks PO qty, GR qty, and invoice qty match). FB60 is free-form — no PO needed. On most projects 60–70% of invoices go through MIRO because most purchases go through procurement.

2. Vendor Master — What a Consultant Configures

In your course you created a vendor with BP. What you may not know is that the vendor master has three distinct data levels, and all must be correctly set before a vendor can be used.

General Data (Client Level)

Name, address, bank details, tax numbers (VAT/TIN). Shared across all Company Codes. If a vendor supplies to multiple entities, one BP is created at client level. Consultant configures: bank account types, tax category fields, duplicate check settings.

Company Code Data (FI View)

Reconciliation Account (e.g. 210100), Payment Terms, Payment Methods, Tolerance Group, Withholding Tax settings. This is the critical AP layer. Without a Reconciliation Account assigned, posting via FB60 will fail with an error.

Purchasing Data (MM View — per Purchasing Org)

Purchasing Terms, Order Currency, Incoterms, Confirmed Delivery Time. Required for MIRO and PO processing. FI consultants work with MM consultants to ensure both views are complete before go-live testing.

⚠️ Classic project mistake: IT creates the vendor at General Data only. AP team posts an invoice in FB60 and gets "Reconciliation account not assigned" error. Always verify Company Code Data is complete for every vendor before UAT starts.

3. Payment Terms — Configuration That Drives Cash Flow

Payment Terms control when invoices are due and whether early payment discounts apply. They are among the most business-critical configurations in AP.

Term Code Description Due Date Logic Config T-Code
0001Payable immediatelyDue = Invoice DateOBB8
0030Net 30 daysDue = Invoice Date + 30OBB8
00142% discount / Net 302% discount if paid in 10 days; otherwise full amount due in 30OBB8
ZENDEnd of month + 30Due = End of invoice month + 30 daysOBB8
  • Payment Terms are configured in OBB8. Each term has a key, description, and due date calculation rules including baseline date logic.
  • Assigned to the vendor in BP (Company Code Data). When an invoice is posted in FB60, SAP reads the term from the vendor master and auto-calculates the due date.
  • Terms can be overridden at the invoice level in FB60 for one-time special agreements — but the default always comes from the vendor master.
  • The Baseline Date starts the clock. Default is the Invoice Date. Can be changed to Posting Date or Document Date per client requirement in OBB8.
Real Scenario: Wrong Due Dates Everywhere

During UAT, the AP team noticed all vendor invoices were due 5 days earlier than expected. Investigation: the Baseline Date was configured as Posting Date instead of Invoice Date. Invoices were being posted 5 days after their invoice date, so the due date was calculated from the posting date and came out short. Fix: change Baseline Date to Invoice Date in OBB8 for all affected payment terms and retest.

4. F110 — Automatic Payment Run (The Most Complex AP Process)

F110 is the most powerful and most misunderstood transaction in AP. Students know it exists. Consultants know exactly how it works and what to do when it fails.

How F110 Selects Invoices
  • You enter a Run Date and Next Payment Run Date. SAP selects all invoices due between today and the next run date — anything due before the next run is included now.
  • Vendor must have a Payment Method assigned (e.g. T = Bank Transfer, C = Check). Only vendors with a matching payment method get selected.
  • The House Bank must be configured with bank details and a GL account assigned. F110 uses this to determine which bank account to pay from.
  • Invoices on Payment Block are excluded. Blocks can be set per invoice in FB60 or on the vendor master in BP.
The 4 Steps of Every F110 Run
1
Proposal Run
SAP simulates the payment run without posting anything. Creates a proposal list showing which invoices will be paid, amounts, and vendors. Review carefully before proceeding.
2
Proposal Edit (Exception Handling)
Review the exception log. Common exceptions: missing bank details on vendor, payment method mismatch, invoice on payment block. Fix the root cause then re-run the proposal.
3
Payment Run (Posting)
Execute the payment. SAP posts payment documents: Dr Vendor / Cr Bank GL. The open invoices are cleared (matched against the payment). Document numbers are assigned.
4
Payment Media / Bank File
Generate the payment file (SEPA, ACH, or local bank format) and send it to the bank. This is configured using FBZP (payment method configuration) and the Payment Medium Workbench (PMW).
⚠️ Critical rule: Never delete a payment run proposal without understanding why. If a vendor is missing from the proposal, check: (1) Is the invoice due within the run date range? (2) Does the vendor have a payment method assigned? (3) Is there a payment block on the invoice or vendor?

5. AP Tolerance Groups — Controlling Invoice Risk

Tolerance Groups in AP control how much difference is acceptable between a purchase order, goods receipt, and invoice. This is separate from GL Tolerance Groups (which control posting amounts).

Vendor Tolerance Group OBA3
  • Max amount allowed per invoice posting for this vendor group
  • Maximum cash discount deviation (e.g. vendor gives 2% but system expects 1.8%)
  • Assigned to vendor in BP Company Code Data view
3-Way Match Tolerances OMR6
  • Maximum acceptable price difference between PO and invoice (e.g. 5% or EGP 500)
  • Maximum acceptable quantity difference
  • If exceeded, MIRO posts the invoice as blocked for payment automatically
Real Scenario: Invoice Blocked After MIRO

Accountant posts a vendor invoice via MIRO. SAP posts it successfully but marks it as "Blocked for Payment." F110 runs but does not pay this vendor. Root cause: the invoice price was 8% above the PO price, exceeding the 5% tolerance configured in OMR6. Fix: either get procurement to revise the PO (ME22N) or release the invoice block manually using MRBR after getting approval.

6. AP Reconciliation — Monthly Control That Proves the Books Are Correct

The most important AP control is ensuring the sub-ledger (FBL1N) exactly matches the reconciliation GL account (210100). Any difference is a posting error that must be investigated.

What a Student Knows

Run FBL1N to see vendor line items. Run FK10N to see vendor balance.

What a Consultant Does Monthly

Sum all vendor balances in FBL1N → compare to GL 210100 balance in FAGLB03 → difference must be zero. Any variance means a manual posting hit the reconciliation account directly — which should be impossible but can happen with incorrect Basis authorizations.

  • FBL1N — Run for all vendors, Open + Cleared items, key date = last day of month. Export to Excel and sum the balance column.
  • FAGLB03 — Display GL account 210100 balance for the same period. The closing balance should match FBL1N total exactly.
  • IDCNAP — AP Aging Report. Shows all open invoices aged into buckets (0–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+ days). Used for cash flow planning and payment prioritization.
  • S_ALR_87012085 — Vendor Balance List. Cross-company vendor balance overview — useful for group-level AP reconciliation.

7. AP Configuration Objects — The 5 Things You Must Know

These are the configuration objects behind every AP posting. When something goes wrong in AP, one of these is always the root cause.

OBB8
Payment Terms
Define payment term calculation rules

If wrong: all invoices for affected vendors get incorrect due dates. F110 may pay too early or too late. Cash discounts may be calculated incorrectly.

FBZP
Payment Methods
Configure payment methods per country and company code

Defines how payments are executed (bank transfer, check, etc.), which bank accounts to use, and which DME format to generate. If wrong: F110 proposal shows no items or generates an incorrect bank file.

OBA3
Vendor Tolerances
Control acceptable posting amounts per vendor group

Sets maximum invoice amounts and allowable cash discount differences. If wrong: large invoices get rejected, or junior users can post unlimited amounts (audit risk).

OMR6
3-Way Match Tol.
Define acceptable PO vs. invoice variance

If the invoice price or quantity differs from the PO by more than the configured tolerance, MIRO blocks the invoice for payment. Set too tight: constant blocks and manual releases. Set too loose: over-payment risk.

OBVU
House Banks
Define company bank accounts used for payments

Links each bank account to a GL account. F110 reads this to determine which GL to credit when making payments. If wrong: F110 has no bank to pay from and the proposal is empty.

8. The 5 AP Errors Every Consultant Faces

Error 1: Vendor not in payment proposal (F110)

Causes: Invoice not yet due / payment method missing on vendor or invoice / payment block set / vendor has no bank details.
Solution: Check proposal exception log — it lists the exact reason for each excluded vendor. Fix the root cause (usually missing payment method in BP or missing bank account in vendor master).

Error 2: Invoice blocked for payment after MIRO

Cause: 3-way match tolerance exceeded (price or quantity variance between PO and invoice). SAP posts the document but sets a payment block automatically.
Solution: Use MRBR (Release Blocked Invoices) to review and manually release after getting business approval. Or ask procurement to adjust the PO in ME22N.

Error 3: FBL1N total ≠ GL 210100 balance

Cause: A manual journal entry (SA document type) was posted directly to account 210100, bypassing the AP sub-ledger. This should be blocked by the reconciliation account setting but occasionally happens with excessive Basis authorizations.
Solution: Find the out-of-sequence posting in FAGLB03 → line item view. Reverse it and repost correctly via FB60.

Error 4: Duplicate invoice posted
