Cause: Same vendor invoice posted twice (different users, or same user twice). SAP has a duplicate check based on Company Code + Vendor + Currency + Amount + Invoice Date + Reference — but it only works if the Reference field was filled consistently.
Solution: Use FBL1N to identify the duplicate. Reverse the second posting with FB08. Set document Reference field as mandatory in field status configuration to prevent recurrence.

Error 5: Cannot reverse an already-paid invoice

Cause: The vendor invoice has been cleared (paid) by F110. Cleared documents cannot be reversed directly in FB08.
Solution: Use FBRA (Reset Clearing) to unlink the payment from the invoice. Then reverse the invoice with FB08. Then re-run or manually handle the payment. Warning: check if the bank payment was already sent — if so, coordinate with treasury before resetting.

AP Consultant T-Code Reference

Essential AP T-codes used on every project — beyond what the basic course covers:

F110Automatic Payment Run
MIROEnter invoice for PO (3-way match)
MRBRRelease blocked invoices
FBZPConfigure payment methods / house banks
OBB8Define payment terms
OBA3Vendor tolerance groups
OMR63-way match tolerance configuration
FBRAReset clearing (before reversal)
IDCNAPAP Aging Report
S_ALR_87012085Vendor balance list
OBVUDefine house banks
ME21NCreate purchase order (MM)
AR CONSULTANT TRACK

Accounts Receivable — The Consultant Layer

AR is the mirror of AP. You collect from customers instead of paying vendors. Learn the full order-to-cash process, configuration objects, and how cash is matched to open invoices.

1. The AR Business Process — Order to Cash (O2C)

AR is driven by the Order-to-Cash cycle — from the moment a sales order is created to the moment the customer pays and the AR is cleared. A consultant must understand every handoff point between SD and FI.

The Order-to-Cash (O2C) Cycle
1
Sales Order — SD VA01
Sales team creates a sales order. No FI posting yet — it is a commitment document in SD. Credit check may trigger at this point based on customer credit limit.
2
Goods Issue (Delivery) — SD VL01N
Warehouse picks and ships goods. SAP auto-posts: Dr Cost of Goods Sold / Cr Inventory. Stock decreases. Accounting impact but still no customer receivable yet.
3
Billing / Invoice — SD to FI VF01
Billing document is created in SD. This automatically generates an FI accounting document: Dr Customer (Recon Account e.g. 110100) / Cr Revenue. The receivable is now live in FI/AR.
4
Manual Invoice (Non-SD) — FI FB70
For invoices not driven by a sales order (e.g. management fee, intercompany recharge, lease income). Direct FI posting: Dr Customer / Cr Revenue.
5
Incoming Payment — FI F-28 / F110
Customer payment arrives. Posted as: Dr Bank GL / Cr Customer. The payment is matched (cleared) against the open invoice. The receivable is cleared and no longer appears in FBL5N as open.
6
AR Month-End Closing
Reconcile FBL5N totals to GL 110100 balance. Run AR aging report (S_ALR_87012168). Send customer statements. Run dunning for overdue items (F150). Close posting period for Customers (account type D) in OB52.
💡 Key integration point: In SD-driven AR, the billing document (VF01) generates the FI document automatically. The SD consultant and FI consultant must agree on account determination (revenue accounts, tax accounts, COGS accounts) during Blueprint — this is called Revenue Account Determination and is configured in transaction VKOA.

2. Customer Master — What a Consultant Configures

Like the vendor master, the customer master (Business Partner) has multiple data levels. Consultants must design and complete all levels before any AR posting can work.

General Data (Client Level)

Name, address, communication, tax IDs. Shared across all Company Codes. A customer that buys from multiple entities has one BP record. Consultant configures: customer duplication check, credit management activation, contact data requirements.

Company Code Data (FI/AR View)

Reconciliation Account (e.g. 110100), Payment Terms, Dunning Procedure, Dunning Block, Tolerance Group, Interest Calculation. Every customer must have a Reconciliation Account — without it, FB70 and VF01 postings will fail.

Sales Area Data (SD View)

Pricing procedure, Incoterms, Sales Currency, Shipping conditions, Credit limit. Required for SD sales orders and billing. FI consultants coordinate with SD consultants on credit limits and payment terms that flow from the sales area into billing.

⚠️ AR-specific risk: The Dunning Procedure and Tolerance Group on the customer master must be configured before go-live. Missing dunning procedure means overdue customers are never chased automatically — a major cash flow risk on any project.

3. Credit Management — Protecting Revenue Before It Becomes a Loss

Credit Management is the AR function that prevents bad debt by checking whether a customer is allowed to receive more goods before they pay for what they already owe.

Static Credit Check
  • Compares customer's total open AR balance against their credit limit
  • If balance exceeds limit: sales order is blocked (credit block)
  • Configuration: FD32 (set customer credit limit), OVA8 (credit check rules)
  • Simple and common on smaller projects
Dynamic Credit Check
  • Considers open orders + deliveries + AR balance vs. credit limit
  • More accurate — catches customers who ordered a lot but not yet delivered
  • Configured with a horizon period (e.g. check open orders next 30 days)
  • Standard on large enterprise implementations
Credit Block Release Process

When a sales order is blocked: (1) SD team sees the block in VKM1 (blocked sales orders). (2) Credit manager reviews the customer's payment history in FBL5N and account balance in FD33. (3) If approved, release the block in VKM4 (release credit hold). (4) Sales order processing continues. Consultant's role: design the approval workflow and authorization matrix — who can release credit blocks and up to what amount.

4. Dunning — The Automated Collections Process

Dunning is SAP's automated process for chasing overdue customers. It replaces manual reminder emails with a controlled, documented, escalating notice system.

How Dunning Works
  • A Dunning Procedure defines the escalation levels (e.g. Level 1 = friendly reminder, Level 2 = final notice, Level 3 = legal warning). Each level has a minimum days overdue and can add interest charges.
  • The dunning procedure is assigned to the customer master (Company Code Data). Without it, the customer is never dunned.
  • F150 is the dunning run transaction. You enter a dunning date and SAP identifies all customers with overdue items, determines the appropriate dunning level, and generates dunning notices.
  • Dunning notices can be sent by email, print, or EDI — configured in the output type settings.
  • A Dunning Block on the customer or invoice prevents it from being included in the dunning run. Common example: invoice under dispute — block it until resolved.
What a Student Knows

Dunning reminds customers to pay. Use F150.

What a Consultant Configures

Define dunning procedure levels in FBMP. Assign procedure to customer master. Configure minimum overdue days per level, interest rates, and notice text. Set dunning blocks for disputed items. Schedule F150 weekly in background jobs.

5. Incoming Payment Clearing — Matching Cash to Invoices

When a customer pays, the key task is clearing the payment against the correct open invoice. This is more complex than it sounds in a real business with hundreds of invoices per customer.

Manual Clearing F-28

Used when the payment advice or bank statement clearly identifies which invoice is being paid. Accountant selects the matching open items and posts the clearing. Dr Bank / Cr Customer. The invoice is cleared.

Automatic Clearing F.13

SAP automatically matches open debits and credits based on matching criteria (amount + reference + assignment field). Useful for clearing intercompany postings or when the payment exactly matches the invoice. Does not work well for partial payments.

Residual Posting (Partial Payment)

Customer pays only part of an invoice. Two approaches: (1) Partial Payment — leaves both items open, links them. (2) Residual Item — clears the original invoice and creates a new open item for the remaining balance. Residual is cleaner for aging reports. Configure in F-28 by selecting "Residual Items" instead of partial.

⚠️ The hardest AR problem in real companies: customers pay the wrong amount or lump multiple invoices into one payment with no clear reference. The AR accountant must investigate FBL5N, contact the customer, and use F-28 or F-32 (Clear Customer) to match correctly. This is called cash application and is often the most time-consuming AR task.

6. AR Reconciliation — Monthly Control

The AR reconciliation check is identical in logic to AP: the FBL5N sub-ledger total must exactly match the GL reconciliation account (110100).

  • FBL5N — Customer Line Item Display. Open Items at Key Date = last day of month. Sum all balances → this is total outstanding AR.
  • FAGLB03 — Display GL account 110100 (Customer Reconciliation) for the same period. Closing balance must match FBL5N total.
  • S_ALR_87012168 — Customer Aging Report (AR equivalent of IDCNAP). Shows overdue AR by aging bucket. Essential for bad debt provisioning and collection prioritization.
  • FD33 — Display Credit Management overview per customer. Shows credit limit, open orders, open deliveries, and total exposure.
  • S_ALR_87012197 — Customer Balance List. All customer balances across the company code for a given period.
Bad Debt Provision — The Year-End AR Task

At year-end, the Finance team must estimate how much of the outstanding AR will not be collected. This is the bad debt provision. Process: (1) Run aging report S_ALR_87012168 to see balances by age. (2) Apply provision rates by age bucket (e.g. 5% for 30–90 days, 25% for 90–180 days, 100% for 180+ days). (3) Post the provision as a GL journal entry: Dr Bad Debt Expense / Cr Provision for Doubtful Debts. (4) When bad debt is confirmed: Dr Provision / Cr Customer (to write off).

7. The 5 AR Errors Every Consultant Faces

Error 1: Customer balance does not match GL 110100

Cause: Manual journal entry posted directly to the customer reconciliation account (110100) via FB50, bypassing FBL5N.
Solution: Find the rogue posting in FAGLB03 → line item view. Reverse and repost correctly via FB70. Tighten authorization to prevent direct posting to reconciliation accounts.

Error 2: Customer payment cannot be applied (no matching invoice)

Cause: Customer paid, but FBL5N shows no matching open item — invoice was not yet posted, or wrong customer account.
Solution: Post the payment as an "On Account" payment (Dr Bank / Cr Customer without clearing). When the invoice is found or posted, clear manually using F-32. Avoid leaving on-account payments uncleared longer than 30 days.

Error 3: Sales order blocked by credit check

Cause: Customer's open AR balance exceeds their credit limit in FD32.
Solution: Review in VKM1 and FD33. If the customer has paid recently but bank statement is not yet reconciled, clear the payment first. If genuinely overdue, escalate to credit manager for VKM4 release decision.

Error 4: Dunning notices sent for paid invoices

Cause: Payment was posted but not cleared against the invoice. Both the invoice and the payment exist as open items. F150 sees the invoice as still open and dunns it.
Solution: Use F-32 to clear the payment against the invoice. Then rerun dunning with corrected status. Prevention: clear incoming payments immediately when posted.

Error 5: AR aging report overstated (old uncleared items)

Cause: Payments were posted as "on account" and never cleared against invoices. FBL5N shows both the original invoices and the uncleared payments as open items — inflating the aged AR and the on-account balance simultaneously.
Solution: Monthly AR housekeeping: use F-32 to clear all matching open debits and credits. Run F.13 automatic clearing for exact matches. Investigate residual items manually.

AR Consultant T-Code Reference

Essential AR T-codes used on every project:

FB70Post customer invoice (non-SD)
F-28Post incoming payment with clearing
F-32Clear open customer items manually
F.13Automatic clearing
FBL5NCustomer line item display
F150Dunning run
FD32Set customer credit limit
FD33Display credit management overview
VKM1Blocked sales orders (credit)
VKM4Release credit blocked orders
FBMPDefine dunning procedures
S_ALR_87012168Customer aging report
CONFIGURATION CONSULTANT TRACK

SAP FI Configuration — The Consultant Layer

Knowing how to use SAP is the end-user layer. Knowing how to build it is the consultant layer. This module covers the full FI configuration sequence — what you configure, why, in what order, and what breaks when you get it wrong.

1. The Configuration Mindset — How a Consultant Thinks

When an end user cannot post a document, they call support. When a consultant cannot post a document, they know exactly which of six configuration objects to check. That gap is what this module closes.

End User Mindset
  • Follows a process someone set up for them
  • When something breaks: asks IT or consultant
  • Knows T-codes, not the rules behind them
  • Works in PRD — the live system only
Consultant Mindset
  • Designs and builds the process
  • When something breaks: reads the error, goes to SPRO, fixes the root cause
  • Knows why T-codes behave the way they do
  • Works in DEV, tests in QAS, deploys to PRD
💡 Every posting error you have ever seen — "Posting period not open", "Field status conflict", "No number range defined" — is caused by a missing or incorrect configuration object. The consultant's job is to know which object, where it lives in SPRO, and how to fix it.

2. The FI Configuration Sequence — What Gets Built First

Configuration must follow a strict order because each step depends on the previous one. You cannot assign a fiscal year variant before you have created the company code. On real projects, this sequence is executed in DEV during the Realize phase.

1
Enterprise Structure OX15 → OX02 → OX16
Define Company → Define Company Code → Assign. Nothing else can exist without a Company Code. This is always Step 1.
2
Fiscal Year Variant OB29 → OB37
Define the fiscal year (Jan–Dec or custom) → Assign to Company Code. Without this, SAP cannot determine which accounting period a posting falls in.
3
Chart of Accounts OB13 → OB62 → OBD4 → OB53
Define COA → Assign to Company Code → Define Account Groups with number ranges → Define Retained Earnings Account. GL accounts cannot be created until the COA exists and is assigned.
4
Posting Periods OBBO → OB37 → OB52
Define variant → Assign to Company Code → Open the current periods. Without this no posting is possible at all — every document will get a "period not open" error.
5
Document Control OBA7 → FBN1
Define document types (SA, KR, KZ, DR, DZ…) and their number ranges. Without number ranges, SAP cannot assign document numbers — posting fails.
6
Field Status OBC4 → OBD2
Define field status groups (which fields are required/optional/suppressed per account) → Assign Field Status Variant to Company Code. Controls what users must fill in when posting.
7
Tolerances OBA4 → OBA0
Define employee tolerance groups (posting limits per user) and GL account tolerances (clearing differences). Then assign users to groups in SU01.
8
Vendor & Customer Account Groups OBD3 → XKN1 / OBD2 → XDN1
Define account groups and number ranges for vendors and customers. Without these, no vendor or customer master can be created in BP.
⚠️ On every project, this sequence is documented in a Configuration Workbook — a spreadsheet that records every decision (which fiscal year, which COA key, which number ranges). Consultants fill this in during the Blueprint phase and use it as the source of truth during Realize.

3. SPRO — How to Navigate the IMG Like a Consultant

Junior consultants get lost in SPRO. Senior consultants navigate it in seconds. The key is knowing the tree structure and memorizing the direct T-codes for the objects you touch most often.

SPRO Path Direct T-Code What it does
Enterprise Structure → Definition → FA → Define CompanyOX15Create the top-level holding company
Enterprise Structure → Definition → FA → Edit Company CodeOX02Create/edit company code (e.g. BEV1)
FA Global Settings → Fiscal Year → Maintain FYVOB29Define fiscal year periods and structure
FA Global Settings → Fiscal Year → Assign FYV to Co. CodeOB37Link fiscal year variant to company code
FA Global Settings → Documents → Posting Periods → Define VariantOBBOCreate posting period variant key
FA Global Settings → Documents → Posting Periods → Open/CloseOB52Open or close periods by account type
GL Accounting → GL Accounts → Preparations → Define COAOB13Create chart of accounts
GL Accounting → GL Accounts → Preparations → Define Account GroupsOBD4Define GL account group number ranges and names
FA Global Settings → Documents → Document TypesOBA7Configure document type properties and allowed account types
FA Global Settings → Documents → Line Item → Field Status VariantsOBC4Define field required/optional/suppressed per account group
Consultant Shortcut: Use Direct T-Codes, Not SPRO Navigation

On real projects, experienced consultants rarely navigate through the full SPRO tree. They know the direct T-code (OB52, OBA7, OBC4) and go straight there. The SPRO tree is for learners and for finding objects you don't know the T-code for. Build a personal cheat sheet of your 20 most-used config T-codes.

4. Transport Requests — Moving Config from DEV to PRD

Every configuration change in DEV is captured in a Transport Request (TR) — a versioned package of changes that travels through DEV → QAS → PRD. This is how SAP enforces the change control principle.

Workbench Request

Carries ABAP development objects — custom programs, enhancements, forms, interfaces. Created and managed by developers. T-code SE09.

Customizing Request

Carries SPRO configuration changes — payment terms, document types, field status groups, posting period variants. Created by consultants every time they save a configuration. T-code SE10 to manage.

The Transport Lifecycle
1
Create: You save a config change in DEV → SAP assigns it to your open TR automatically (or you create a new one).
2
Release: In SE10, you release the TR. Once released it is locked — no more changes can be added to it. It is ready to import into QAS.
3
Import to QAS: The Basis team imports the TR into QAS. Key users test the change. Issues go back to DEV for correction (new TR).
4
Import to PRD: After sign-off on QAS, the TR is imported into PRD at go-live (or during a change window). The configuration is now live.
⚠️ Never click "Local Object" when prompted during configuration. Local objects are never transported — they exist only in DEV and will be missing from QAS and PRD. This is a classic junior consultant mistake that causes UAT failures.

5. Global Parameters — The Company Code Settings Screen

T-code OBY6 (or via SPRO → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Company Code → Enter Global Parameters) is the central settings screen for a company code. It ties together all the configuration steps.

  • Chart of Accounts — which COA this company code uses (set here from OB62)
  • Fiscal Year Variant — which fiscal year applies (set here from OB37)
  • Posting Period Variant — which period control variant applies
  • Field Status Variant — which field status rules apply
  • Country, Currency, Language — the legal and operational settings of this entity
  • Credit Control Area — which credit management area controls this company code's customers
💡 Think of OBY6 as the "summary screen" that shows everything assigned to a company code. On a new project, consultants check OBY6 first to verify all required assignments are in place before starting UAT.

6. The 7 Configuration Errors Every FI Consultant Faces on Day One

Error 1: "Posting period X/YYYY is not open"

Cause: Period is closed in OB52 for the relevant account type, or the posting period variant is not assigned to the company code.
Fix: Go to OB52 → find the Posting Period Variant → open the period for the correct account type (+ for all, or K/D/S for specific types).
Prevention: Agree a period calendar with the client and set a recurring task to open/close periods on the 1st of each month.

Error 2: "No number range defined for document type SA in year 20XX"

Cause: Number ranges in FBN1 are defined per year. If you go live in 2025 and your number range was defined for 2024 only, posting fails on January 1st, 2025.
Fix: Go to FBN1 → select document type → extend or copy the number range to the new year.
Prevention: At year-end, run SNRO or manually copy number ranges for all document types to the new fiscal year. Add this to the year-end closing checklist.

Error 3: "Field status conflict — field Cost Center is required/suppressed"

Cause: The field status group on the GL account and the field status of the posting key conflict. One says "Required", the other says "Suppressed".
Fix: Go to OBC4 → find the Field Status Group assigned to the account (check in FS00) → change the conflicting field to Optional in both the group and the posting key.
Note: SAP applies the more restrictive of the two — Required vs Suppressed cannot be resolved. Always use Optional as the safe middle ground.

Error 4: "You are not authorized to post an amount of X"

Cause: The user's Tolerance Group in OBA4 has a maximum document amount that the invoice exceeds.
Fix: Either increase the tolerance limit for this user group in OBA4 (requires authorization from Financial Controller) or have a senior user post it.
Consultant note: Never increase tolerance limits without written approval. Tolerance groups are an audit control — changes must be documented.

Error 5: "Account X is a reconciliation account — direct posting not permitted"

Cause: In FS00, the GL account has Reconciliation Account type set (K = Vendors, D = Customers, A = Assets). Direct posting to these accounts is blocked by design — they must only be posted through the sub-ledger transactions (FB60 for vendors, FB70 for customers).
Fix: Use the correct transaction. Never try to post directly to a reconciliation account via FB50 or F-02.

Error 6: "Company code X does not have a chart of accounts assigned"

Cause: Step 3 of the configuration sequence (OB62 — Assign COA to Company Code) was skipped or done incorrectly.
Fix: Go to OB62 → find the company code → assign the correct COA. Also verify in OBY6 that the global parameters are complete.

Error 7: Transport imported to PRD but config is missing

Cause: The consultant saved the configuration as "Local Object" instead of assigning it to a Transport Request. Local objects never leave DEV.
Fix: Recreate the configuration in DEV and this time assign it to a TR. Release and import the TR to QAS and PRD.
Prevention: Never click "Local Object". Always assign to a project Transport Request. Verify in SE10 that your TR contains the expected objects before releasing.

7. The Three Layers — Configuration, Master Data, Transactions

A consultant must always think in three layers. Confusing them is the most common conceptual mistake made by new FI consultants.

Layer 1
Configuration
  • Done in SPRO by the consultant
  • Defines the rules and structure
  • Examples: Document types, field status groups, fiscal year variant, posting period rules
  • Done once during project setup; rarely changed after go-live
  • Transported via TR (DEV→QAS→PRD)
Layer 2
Master Data
  • Done by key users or consultants
  • Defines the entities the business uses
  • Examples: GL accounts (FS00), vendor masters (BP), customer masters, cost centers
  • Created before go-live; updated ongoing
  • Usually migrated via LSMW or data upload tools
Layer 3
Transactions
  • Done by end users daily
  • The actual business postings
  • Examples: FB60 (vendor invoice), FB50 (GL entry), F110 (payment run)
  • Ongoing; generates the financial data
  • Works only if Layers 1 and 2 are correctly set up
💡 Real example: A user tries to post an invoice in FB60 and gets an error. The consultant's diagnosis flow: Is the posting period open? (Layer 1 — OB52). Does the vendor master exist with a reconciliation account? (Layer 2 — BP). Are the correct GL accounts available in the COA? (Layer 1 + 2). Only after both layers are confirmed does the consultant look at the transaction itself.

Configuration Consultant T-Code Reference

The complete FI configuration T-code toolkit — organized by configuration area:

Enterprise Structure
OX15Define Company (holding entity)
OX02Define / edit Company Code
OX16Assign Company Code to Company
OBY6Global Parameters — summary of all assignments
Fiscal Year & Periods
OB29Maintain Fiscal Year Variant
OB37Assign Fiscal Year Variant to Co. Code
OBBODefine Posting Period Variant
OB52Open and Close Posting Periods
Chart of Accounts & GL
OB13Define Chart of Accounts
OB62Assign COA to Company Code
OBD4Define GL Account Groups
OB53Define Retained Earnings Account
Documents & Field Control
OBA7Define Document Types
FBN1Define Document Number Ranges
OBC4Maintain Field Status Groups
OBD2Assign Field Status Variant to Co. Code
Tolerances & Sub-Ledger Setup
OBA4Employee Tolerance Groups
OBA0GL Account Tolerance Groups
OBD3Vendor Account Groups
XKN1Vendor Number Ranges
OBD2Customer Account Groups
XDN1Customer Number Ranges
Transport Management
SE09Transport Organizer (create, release TRs)
SE10Transport Organizer (manage customizing TRs)
SPROImplementation Guide (full config tree)

8. Document Types — The Rules Behind Every Posting

Every posting in SAP carries a Document Type — a two-character key that controls what that document is allowed to do. When a posting fails with "Document type XX not defined" or "Account type not permitted," the root cause is always here.

What a Document Type Controls (OBA7)
Number Range
Each document type is linked to a number range (defined in FBN1). When a document posts, SAP assigns the next available number from that range. If the number range is exhausted or missing, the posting fails immediately. Number ranges can be internal (SAP assigns) or external (the user enters).
Account Types Allowed
Controls which account types can appear as line items in documents of this type. The types are: A = Assets, D = Customers, K = Vendors, S = GL Accounts, M = Materials. Example: document type KR (Vendor Invoice) allows K + S. If you try to post an asset line in a KR document, SAP rejects it.
Reverse Document Type
When you reverse a document (FB08), SAP uses the Reverse Document Type defined here to create the reversal. Example: reversing a KR document creates an KG (Vendor Credit Memo) document. If this field is blank, the reversal uses the same document type as the original.
Net Document Type
Controls whether the document posts gross (tax as a separate line) or net (tax included in the expense line). Relevant for VAT-registered companies. A GL accountant never sees this setting but is always affected by it.
Reference / Header Text
Whether the Reference and Header Text fields are required, optional, or suppressed when entering a document of this type. Setting Reference as required on KR documents is the standard way to enforce the vendor invoice number field — enabling SAP's duplicate invoice detection.
Standard Document Types — What Every FI Consultant Must Know
Type Description Account Types Typical T-Code Number Range
SAGL Account DocumentS onlyFB50, F-021xxxxxxx
ABAccounting DocumentAll typesF-02 (generic)1xxxxxxx
KRVendor InvoiceK + SFB60, MIRO19xxxxxxx
KGVendor Credit MemoK + SFB6519xxxxxxx
KZVendor PaymentK + SF-53, F11015xxxxxxx
DRCustomer InvoiceD + SFB7018xxxxxxx
DGCustomer Credit MemoD + SFB7518xxxxxxx
DZCustomer PaymentD + SF-2814xxxxxxx
ZPPayment (Auto)K + D + SF11015xxxxxxx
AAAsset PostingA + SF-90, ABZONvarious
Number Ranges — FBN1

Number ranges are defined per company code in FBN1. Each range has a number interval (e.g. 1900000000–1999999999), a current number (the last assigned), and an internal/external flag. A range must be defined for every document type used in that company code before any posting is possible.

Internal Number Range

SAP automatically assigns the next sequential document number. The user never sees or enters the number — it is generated on post. This is standard for all FI document types. Never runs out in practice — ranges typically hold 10 billion numbers.

External Number Range

The user must manually enter the document number. Used when the company wants document numbers to match an external system (e.g. legacy system invoice numbers during migration). If the user does not enter a number, or enters a duplicate, posting fails.

The 3 Most Common Document Type Errors (and their fixes)
"Posting key X not defined for document type Y" — The account type for that posting key is not allowed in the document type. Check OBA7 → open the document type → review which account types are ticked. Add the missing type or use the correct document type.
"No document number range defined for document type KR in company code BEV1" — Go to FBN1, select company code BEV1, find the number range group for KR, and create the interval if missing. This is the #1 error on a fresh company code setup.
"Number range interval exhausted" — The current number has reached the end of the interval. In FBN1, extend the interval to a higher end value. On production systems this rarely happens but must be monitored annually — set an alert at 80% utilisation.

9. Field Status Groups — Why Some Fields Are Mandatory and Others Aren't

When a user posts in FB50 or FB60, some fields appear as required (red asterisk), some as optional, and some don't appear at all. This behavior is controlled entirely by the Field Status Group (FSG) assigned to the GL account in FS00. Without understanding FSGs, you cannot explain or fix field-related posting errors.

The Field Status Concept — Three States
Required
Field appears with a red asterisk. Must be filled in or posting is blocked. Example: Cost Center on all P&L accounts.
Optional
Field appears but is not mandatory. User can fill it or leave it blank. Example: Assignment field, Reference.
Suppressed
Field is completely hidden — does not appear on the posting screen at all. Example: Order field on bank accounts.
How Field Status Works — The Two Sources

The field status for any given posting line is determined by two sources combined — SAP takes the stricter of the two:

Source 1
OBC4 → FSG
GL Account Field Status Group — defined in FS00 (Create/Bank/Interest tab → Field Status Group). The FSG is a key (e.g. G001, G004, G029) that points to a group maintained in OBC4. This group defines which fields are required/optional/suppressed for this specific GL account.
Source 2
Posting Key
Posting Key Field Status — each posting key (40, 50, 31, 15…) also carries its own field status settings. This is rarely changed on projects but it is the second source SAP checks. The combination rule: if either source says Required, the field is Required. If one says Suppressed but the other says Required, SAP shows an error — this is the infamous "Field status conflict" error.
Standard Field Status Groups — OBC4
FSG Key Typical Use Cost Center Assignment Tax Code
G001General GL accountsOptionalOptionalOptional
G004Bank / Cash accountsSuppressedRequiredSuppressed
G005Expense / Cost accounts (with CC)RequiredOptionalOptional
G029Reconciliation accounts (AP/AR)SuppressedOptionalOptional
G067Tax accountsSuppressedSuppressedRequired
How to Configure FSGs in OBC4 — Step by Step
1
Navigate to OBC4 — SPRO → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Document → Line Item → Controls → Maintain Field Status Variants. Select the Field Status Variant assigned to your company code (usually the same key as the company code, e.g. BEV1 or INT).
2
Open a Field Status Group — Double-click an FSG key (e.g. G005). A list of field groups appears: General Data, Additional Account Assignments, Materials Management, Asset Accounting, etc.
3
Double-click a group to set field statuses — For example, open "Additional Account Assignments" to control Cost Center, Profit Center, Internal Order. Set Cost Center to Required for expense accounts, Suppressed for balance sheet accounts.
4
Assign the FSG to accounts in FS00 — Open each GL account in FS00 → Create/Bank/Interest tab → Field Status Group field. Enter the correct FSG key (e.g. G005 for expense accounts). Save and transport.
The 3 Field Status Errors Every Consultant Sees
"Field Cost Center is a required field for account 501000 / BEV1" — The FSG on this account (G005) has Cost Center = Required. The user did not enter a cost center. Fix for the user: enter a cost center. Fix for the consultant (if the account should not need a CC): change the FSG on FS00 or update OBC4 to make Cost Center optional.
"Field status conflict between posting key and account" — One source sets a field as Suppressed and the other sets it as Required. SAP cannot satisfy both at once. Fix: check the FSG in OBC4 and the posting key field status, make them consistent. Change Suppressed to Optional in the source that is wrong.
Client asks: "Why does Cost Center not appear when posting to the bank account but it does on expense accounts?" — Bank accounts use FSG G004 which suppresses Cost Center (bank transactions don't belong to a cost center). Expense accounts use G005 which requires it. This is by design. This is the question every Blueprint client asks in week 2 of the project — and now you can answer it in one sentence.
💡 Project tip: During the Blueprint, the consultant produces a Field Status Matrix — a spreadsheet listing every GL account group and which fields are required/optional/suppressed. The client's key users sign off on this. It becomes the configuration specification for OBC4. Getting this wrong after go-live means fixing live postings and is very painful.
BANK ACCOUNTING CONSULTANT TRACK

Bank Accounting — The Consultant Layer

Bank Accounting connects the SAP system to the real world of cash. Every payment you process and every bank statement you receive passes through this module. Learn the full cycle — from house bank setup to bank reconciliation — the way a consultant designs and supports it.

1. Bank Accounting in the FI Landscape

Bank Accounting in SAP FI is not a standalone module — it is the settlement layer that completes AP payments, AR collections, and GL cash transactions. Every payment run (F110) and every manual payment posts to a Bank GL account defined in the House Bank configuration.

What feeds INTO Bank Accounting
  • AP payment run (F110) — Dr Vendor / Cr Bank GL
  • Manual AP payment (F-53) — same posting
  • AR incoming payment (F-28) — Dr Bank GL / Cr Customer
  • Petty cash (FBCJ) — Dr/Cr Cash GL
  • Manual bank transfers (FB50, F-02)
What comes OUT of Bank Accounting
  • Bank GL account balance — the book balance of cash
  • Bank statement postings — reconciling book to bank
  • Cleared items — matched payments and receipts
  • Bank charges posted automatically
  • Exchange rate differences on foreign currency accounts

2. House Bank Setup — The Foundation of All Payments

A House Bank represents a real bank account your company holds. SAP connects it to a GL account so that every payment automatically posts to the correct cash account. Without House Banks, F110 cannot run and no outgoing payments are possible.

1
Define House Bank FI12
Create the House Bank ID (e.g. HSBC01), enter the bank's country, routing number/SWIFT/IBAN. This is the master record for the bank itself. Path: SPRO → Financial Accounting → Bank Accounting → Bank Accounts.
2
Define Account ID (Bank Subaccount) FI12
Under each House Bank, define one or more Account IDs — each representing a specific bank account number (e.g. checking account, USD account, EGP account). Each Account ID is linked to a GL account in FS00.
3
Link to GL Account
Each Account ID is assigned a GL account (e.g. account 113000 — CIB Bank EGP). When F110 pays a vendor, SAP credits this exact GL account. The GL account must be created in FS00 first with account type "Balance Sheet" and no reconciliation account type.
4
Assign to Payment Methods FBZP
In FBZP (Automatic Payment Configuration), assign the House Bank to each payment method (Bank Transfer, Check, etc.) and define ranking when multiple banks exist. F110 reads this to determine which bank to pay from.
⚠️ Common mistake: Creating the House Bank in FI12 but forgetting to assign it in FBZP. F110 proposal will be empty — SAP has no bank to pay from. Always verify the full chain: FI12 → GL account in FS00 → FBZP assignment.

3. Bank Statement Processing — Reconciling Book to Bank

The bank statement is the bank's record of what actually moved in/out of your account. SAP compares it against the book balance in the Bank GL account. Any difference is an unreconciled item that must be investigated.

Manual Bank Statement FF67
  • Accountant manually enters bank statement line by line
  • SAP matches each line against open items in FBL3N
  • Matched items are cleared automatically
  • Unmatched lines post to an interim clearing account
  • Used when no electronic bank feed is available
Electronic Bank Statement FF_5
  • Bank sends a file (MT940, CAMT.053, BAI2 format)
  • SAP imports it automatically via FF_5 or FEBP
  • Matching rules configured in SPRO (transaction types → posting rules)
  • Standard for large enterprises — reduces manual work
  • Unmatched items go to a suspense/clearing account
The Bank Reconciliation Control

At month-end, the Bank GL balance in FAGLB03 should equal the bank's closing balance on the statement. Any difference = unreconciled items. The accountant investigates using FBL3N (open items on the bank GL account). Common causes: payment posted in SAP but not yet cleared by bank (outstanding cheque), or bank charges not yet posted in SAP.

4. Cash Journal — FBCJ

The Cash Journal (FBCJ) is used for petty cash transactions — small cash payments and receipts that do not go through the bank. It has its own two-step process: Save (draft) → Post Selected (actual GL posting).

  • Each company code can have multiple Cash Journals — one per physical cash box or branch.
  • The Cash Journal is linked to a GL cash account (e.g. account 100000 — Petty Cash). Configured in SPRO: FBCJC1 (Set Up Cash Journal).
  • Business transactions defined in FBCJC2 (e.g. "Cash Expense", "Cash Revenue", "Salary Advance") — each maps to a specific GL account so the accountant just selects the transaction type.
  • FBCJ enforces a daily balance — you cannot have a negative cash balance. This is an internal control.
  • Reports: FBCJR (Cash Journal Report) shows all daily transactions with running balance.
What a Student knows

FBCJ has Save and Post Selected. Cash entries post to the petty cash GL.

What a Consultant configures

FBCJC1 to create and assign GL accounts to each Cash Journal. FBCJC2 to define the business transaction types. Limits per transaction. Currency settings. Print layout for cash vouchers.

5. Bank Accounting Month-End Closing

1
Process all bank statements
Import or manually enter the final bank statement for the month. All cleared items must be matched before closing.
2
Post bank charges and interest FB50
Any bank service charges, interest income, or interest expense appearing on the statement must be posted manually if not covered by bank statement rules.
3
Foreign currency revaluation FAGL_FC_VAL
If the company holds foreign currency bank accounts, revalue the balance at the month-end exchange rate. SAP posts the difference as unrealized FX gain/loss.
4
Reconcile Bank GL to statement FAGLB03 / FBL3N
Bank GL balance in SAP must equal the bank's closing balance. Investigate all open items. Produce the bank reconciliation report before period close.
5
Close posting period for bank accounts OB52
Once reconciliation is complete and reviewed, close the period for GL accounts (account type S) to prevent late postings to the bank account.

6. The 4 Bank Accounting Errors Every Consultant Faces

Error 1: F110 proposal is empty — no vendors selected

Cause: House Bank not assigned to the payment method in FBZP, or the vendor has no payment method assigned in BP.
Fix: Check FBZP → Paying Company Codes → Bank Determination → rank the House Bank for the payment method. Verify vendor master has a payment method (T for transfer, C for cheque) in BP Company Code Data.

Error 2: Bank GL balance ≠ bank statement closing balance

Cause: Outstanding payments (issued but not yet cleared by bank), outstanding deposits (received but not yet on statement), or bank charges not yet posted in SAP.
Fix: Run FBL3N for the Bank GL account with "Open Items" — any open item is an unreconciled transaction. Investigate each one. Post bank charges if missing.

Error 3: Payment posted to wrong bank account

Cause: FBZP bank ranking assigned the wrong House Bank or Account ID for the payment method. F110 paid from the USD account instead of the EGP account.
Fix: Go to FBZP → Bank Determination → correct the account ranking. For the already-posted wrong payment: if not yet sent to bank, reverse with FBRA + FB08 and redo. If already sent to bank, handle via manual journal and coordinate with treasury.

Error 4: FBCJ shows negative balance — system blocks posting

Cause: More cash was posted as paid out than is physically available in the cash journal. SAP enforces a non-negative cash balance as an internal control.
Fix: Post a cash receipt first (petty cash replenishment) to increase the balance, then the expense can be posted. Review with the Finance team whether the physical cash was genuinely available.

Bank Accounting T-Code Reference

FI12Define House Bank and Account IDs
FBZPAutomatic Payment configuration (payment methods, house banks)
F110Automatic Payment Run
F-53Manual outgoing payment (AP)
F-28Manual incoming payment (AR)
FF67Manual bank statement entry
FF_5Import electronic bank statement
FEBPPost electronic bank statement
FBCJCash Journal — petty cash transactions
FBCJC1Set up Cash Journal (config)
FBCJC2Define Cash Journal business transactions
FAGL_FC_VALForeign currency revaluation
FAGLB03GL account balance display (bank reconciliation)
FBL3NGL line items — open items on bank GL account
ASSET ACCOUNTING CONSULTANT TRACK

Asset Accounting (FI-AA) — The Consultant Layer

Asset Accounting tracks the value of everything your company owns — from office equipment to machinery to vehicles. It manages acquisition, depreciation, transfer, and retirement of assets, and feeds the balance sheet and P&L automatically.

1. What Is Asset Accounting?

FI-AA (Financial Accounting — Asset Accounting) is the SAP sub-module that manages fixed assets throughout their entire lifecycle. It is tightly integrated with FI-GL — every asset transaction automatically generates a GL posting to the asset balance sheet accounts and depreciation expense accounts.

🏭
Acquisition

Asset is purchased or created. Value is capitalised on the balance sheet. Dr Asset / Cr Bank or Vendor.

📉
Depreciation

Monthly or annual reduction in asset value. Dr Depreciation Expense / Cr Accumulated Depreciation.

🗑️
Retirement

Asset is sold or scrapped. Net book value removed from balance sheet. Gain/loss posted to P&L.

2. Asset Accounting Configuration — The Setup Every Consultant Needs to Know

Before any asset can be created or depreciated, a chain of configuration must be in place. This is done once per project but must be understood by every FI consultant.

OADB
Depreciation Areas
Define Depreciation Areas

A Depreciation Area defines a set of rules for valuing the asset — typically one for local GAAP (book), one for tax purposes, and one for IFRS. Area 01 is always the leading area that posts to GL. Others can run in parallel without GL postings.

OAOA
Asset Classes
Define Asset Classes

Asset Classes group assets by type (e.g. 1000 = Buildings, 2000 = Machinery, 3000 = Vehicles, 4000 = Furniture). Each class assigns default depreciation keys, useful life, and GL accounts. Every asset created must belong to an asset class.

AO90
Account Determination
Assign GL Accounts to Asset Classes

For each asset class and each depreciation area, define which GL accounts receive the postings: Asset Cost Account, Accumulated Depreciation Account, Depreciation Expense Account, Gain/Loss on Sale accounts. This is the critical link between FI-AA and FI-GL.

AFAMA
Depreciation Keys
Define Depreciation Keys

A Depreciation Key combines the depreciation method (straight-line, declining balance) with the period control (how to prorate in the first and last year). Common keys: LINR (straight-line), DEGR (declining balance), MANU (manual). Assigned to the asset class as a default.

3. Asset Master Record — Creating an Asset

Every physical asset must have an Asset Master Record before any transaction can be posted against it. Created in AS01.

General Tab
  • Asset Class (mandatory — drives all GL accounts)
  • Company Code (mandatory)
  • Asset Description (short and long text)
  • Serial number, inventory number
  • Cost Center (for depreciation cost allocation)
Depreciation Areas Tab
  • Depreciation Key (e.g. LINR for straight-line)
  • Useful Life in years (e.g. 5 years for a laptop)
  • Ordinary depreciation start date
  • Scrap value at end of useful life
  • Each depreciation area can have different values
💡 AS01 creates a "shell" asset with no value. The value is only added when an acquisition posting is made (AS91 for data migration of existing assets, or F-90/MIGO for new purchases). Think of AS01 as creating the account — the balance comes from the posting.

4. Asset Transactions — The Full Lifecycle

Transaction T-Code GL Posting Notes
Create Asset MasterAS01No GL postingCreates the shell record only
Acquisition (external purchase)F-90Dr Asset / Cr VendorFor PO-based: MIGO GR posts asset
Acquisition (in-house production)AIABDr Asset / Cr WIP GLSettles a construction-in-progress order
Depreciation RunAFABDr Dep. Expense / Cr Acc. DepreciationRun monthly or annually; test run first
Asset TransferABUMNMoves NBV between asset master recordsUsed when asset changes cost center or class
Retirement (sale)F-92Dr Bank/Customer + Acc.Dep / Cr Asset + Gain/LossRemoves asset from books; posts gain or loss
Retirement (scrapping)ABAVNDr Acc.Depreciation + Loss / Cr AssetNo proceeds — full net book value to loss
Data migration (legacy assets)AS91Special migration postingUsed at go-live to load opening balances

5. Depreciation — What Every FI Consultant Must Understand

Depreciation is the systematic reduction of an asset's value over its useful life. In SAP, depreciation is calculated automatically based on the depreciation key and useful life on the asset master. The consultant's job is to configure the right keys and run the depreciation program.

Straight-Line (LINR)

Equal amount each year. Asset value: 100,000. Useful life: 5 years. Annual depreciation: 20,000/year. NBV at end: 0 (or scrap value). Most common method. Simple to audit.

Declining Balance (DEGR)

Higher depreciation in early years, decreasing over time. E.g. 40% of NBV each year: Year 1: 40,000. Year 2: 24,000. Year 3: 14,400. Common for machinery that loses value quickly at the start.

The AFAB Depreciation Run — Monthly Process

T-code AFAB runs depreciation for all assets in a company code for a specified period. Always run in Test Mode first — this simulates without posting. Review the output for any errors (asset with missing useful life, negative NBV, etc.). If clean, run in Post Mode to generate the actual GL documents. Run AFAB before closing the period — depreciation must be posted before month-end close.

⚠️ AFAB cannot be reversed easily. If you run it in Post Mode with wrong settings, you need to run a repeat depreciation (AFAB with "Repeat" flag) and then investigate each incorrect asset. Always use Test Mode first — this is non-negotiable on a live project.

6. Asset Reporting — What the Finance Team Uses

  • S_ALR_87011990 — Asset Balances Report. Shows gross value, accumulated depreciation, and net book value for all assets by asset class. The standard balance sheet report for fixed assets.
  • S_ALR_87011982 — Asset History Sheet. Full year-to-date movement: opening balance, additions, retirements, depreciation, closing balance. Required for statutory reporting and audit.
  • S_ALR_87012026 — Depreciation Forecast. Shows projected depreciation for future periods. Used for budget planning and cash flow forecasting.
  • AW01N — Asset Explorer. The single most useful asset T-code — shows the complete history of one asset: all postings, depreciation per period, planned vs posted, all areas side by side. Used for troubleshooting individual assets.
What a Student knows

Assets are created in AS01 and depreciation runs in AFAB.

What a Consultant knows

AW01N to diagnose any asset issue in 60 seconds. S_ALR_87011982 for the complete asset movement schedule. AFAB in test mode before every post. AS91 for data migration. AO90 for account determination when GL links are wrong.

7. Asset Accounting Year-End Process

Year-end in FI-AA follows a specific sequence. Missing a step causes the next fiscal year to open incorrectly or depreciation to be wrong.

1
Run final depreciation for period 12 AFAB
Post all depreciation for December before year-end close. Verify no assets have unposted depreciation (use AFAR — Recalculate Values if needed).
2
Reconcile asset sub-ledger to GL ABST2
ABST2 checks that all asset postings match the GL account balances. Any discrepancy must be resolved before year-end close. A clean ABST2 is a prerequisite for year-end.
3
Close fiscal year in Asset Accounting AJRW
AJRW closes the old fiscal year for FI-AA. After this, no more postings to the prior year are possible. Opens the new fiscal year in Asset Accounting.
4
Open new fiscal year for Asset Accounting AJAB
AJAB officially opens the new fiscal year and carries forward asset values. All asset NBVs from year-end become opening balances for the new year.
5
GL balance carryforward FAGLGVTR
The same GL year-end close that carries forward all balance sheet accounts — including asset and accumulated depreciation accounts — to the new year.

8. The 4 Asset Accounting Errors Every Consultant Faces

Error 1: AFAB runs but no GL documents are generated

Cause: Depreciation Area 01 is not set to post to GL (posting indicator = "0" instead of "1" in OADB).
Fix: Go to OADB → find Depreciation Area 01 → change posting indicator to "1" (posts in real-time) or "2" (posts periodically). This change requires a transport and impacts all future AFAB runs.

Error 2: Asset acquisition posts but wrong GL accounts hit

Cause: Account determination (AO90) is missing or incorrectly configured — wrong GL account assigned to the asset class for the transaction type.
Fix: Go to AO90 → find the account determination key for the asset class → correct the GL accounts for acquisition, depreciation, and retirement. Also check that the GL accounts in FS00 are marked as reconciliation account type "A" (Assets).

Error 3: Cannot post to prior year after AJRW was run

Cause: AJRW closed the FI-AA fiscal year. Asset Accounting does not allow prior-year postings once the year is closed, even if the FI posting period (OB52) is still open.
Fix: This cannot easily be reversed. If the prior-year posting is essential, consult SAP Notes for "reopening" procedures — this requires Basis involvement and must be done very carefully with client CFO approval.

Error 4: ABST2 shows discrepancy between asset sub-ledger and GL

Cause: Manual GL posting was made directly to an asset GL account (bypassing FI-AA) or a failed AFAB left partial postings.
Fix: Use ABST to display the difference in detail. Identify the out-of-sync document. If it was a manual GL posting to an asset account: reverse it and repost correctly through FI-AA. Never post directly to asset balance sheet accounts via FB50.

Asset Accounting T-Code Reference

Configuration
OADBDefine Depreciation Areas
OAOADefine Asset Classes
AO90Assign GL accounts to asset classes
AFAMADefine Depreciation Keys
Master Data & Transactions
AS01Create Asset Master Record
AS02Change Asset Master Record
AS03Display Asset Master Record
AS91Create old asset (data migration)
F-90Asset acquisition from vendor
ABUMNAsset transfer within company code
F-92Asset retirement with sale proceeds
ABAVNAsset retirement by scrapping
Depreciation & Year-End
AFABRun depreciation (test + post)
AFARRecalculate asset values
ABST2Reconcile asset sub-ledger to GL
AJRWClose fiscal year in Asset Accounting
AJABOpen new fiscal year for assets
Reporting
AW01NAsset Explorer — full asset history
S_ALR_87011990Asset Balances (NBV by class)
S_ALR_87011982Asset History Sheet (movements)
S_ALR_87012026Depreciation Forecast
